[지문 260]

A young entrepreneur, Maria, was devastated after her first business venture failed. She confessed to her mentor, an experienced businessman named David, that she felt like a complete failure and was afraid to try anything new. David listened patiently and then shared stories of his own early struggles, including two bankruptcies. He told her, "Success is not a clean, straight line. It's a messy, winding path. Don't hide from your failure; study it. Understand what went wrong and why. You must learn to see that a master's scars are his medals." He encouraged her to view her recent experience not as a final judgment, but as the most valuable part of her education.

Vocabulary

  • devastated엄청난 충격을 받은
  • venture벤처 사업
  • winding구불구불한
  • scars흉터
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 261]

In the mid-19th century, the German states were a fragmented collection of territories. Liberals and nationalists had long dreamed of a unified Germany, but their attempts through debate and diplomacy in the Frankfurt Parliament of 1848 had failed. It was in this context that Otto von Bismarck, the Minister President of Prussia, delivered his famous speech in 1862. He argued that the great questions of the day would not be settled by speeches and majority decisions—that was the great mistake of 1848 and 1849. Instead, he declared, they would be decided “by iron and blood.” This statement signaled a dramatic shift in the approach to German unification, moving away from idealism and towards a more pragmatic, if ruthless, form of statecraft.

Vocabulary

  • fragmented분열된
  • diplomacy외교
  • ruthless무자비한
  • statecraft국정 운영 기술
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 262]

Franz Kafka's novella *The Metamorphosis* begins with its protagonist, Gregor Samsa, waking up to find himself transformed into a monstrous insect. Initially, his family reacts with shock and distress. However, as Gregor becomes unable to work and support them, their feelings curdle into resentment and disgust. He is confined to his room, and his presence becomes a shameful secret. The family's financial struggles and the social stigma they face because of him lead them to view him not as their son and brother, but as a burden. His sister, Grete, who was once his closest confidante, eventually declares that they must find a way to dispose of him, stating that "this creature" is no longer Gregor. For the family, he had become “an affliction that had to be shaken off.”

Vocabulary

  • protagonist주인공
  • curdle엉기다, 굳어지다
  • stigma오명
  • affliction고통
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 263]

In his *Meditations on First Philosophy*, René Descartes embarks on a quest for absolute certainty. To achieve this, he employs a method of radical doubt, questioning everything he once believed. He considers the possibility that his senses deceive him and that even the truths of mathematics could be illusions. To push this doubt to its absolute limit, he introduces a powerful thought experiment. He hypothesizes the existence of not God, who is supremely good, but of a supremely powerful and cunning "evil demon" who is dedicated to deceiving him at every turn. Descartes does not actually believe in this entity; rather, the figure of “this malicious demon” serves a specific philosophical purpose. It is a tool designed to sustain his doubt and prevent him from lapsing back into his old, unexamined opinions.

Vocabulary

  • embarkson ~에 착수하다
  • radical근본적인
  • hypothesizes가설을 세우다
  • lapsingback 되돌아가는 것
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 264]

Groupthink is a psychological phenomenon that occurs within a group of people in which the desire for harmony or conformity results in an irrational or dysfunctional decision-making outcome. Cohesive groups, especially those with a strong, persuasive leader, are particularly vulnerable. Dissenting opinions are discouraged, and individuals tend to self-censor to avoid disrupting the group's apparent consensus. In such an environment, alternative strategies are not fully explored, and potential risks are overlooked. The group members share an illusion of invulnerability and an unquestioned belief in the group's morality. As critical thinking gives way to conformity, “the meeting room becomes an echo chamber,” where the same ideas are repeated and amplified, while contradictory evidence is ignored or rationalized away.

Vocabulary

  • conformity순응
  • dysfunctional역기능적인
  • self-censor자기 검열하다
  • invulnerability무적
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 265]

The rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AI) presents humanity with a profound dilemma. On one hand, AI promises unprecedented benefits: curing diseases, solving complex environmental problems, and automating tedious labor. On the other hand, it carries immense risks, such as mass unemployment, autonomous weapons, and the potential for a superintelligence that could escape human control. Critics warn that we are rushing forward without fully understanding the consequences. They argue that creating something far more intelligent than ourselves is a monumental gamble. For these skeptics, the pursuit of artificial general intelligence is akin to “opening Pandora’s box.” Once it is done, there may be no way to reverse the unforeseen and potentially catastrophic outcomes that follow.

Vocabulary

  • dilemma딜레마
  • tedious지루한
  • superintelligence초지능
  • akinto ~와 유사한
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 266]

In 1917, the art world was scandalized by a work submitted to an exhibition of the Society of Independent Artists. The piece, titled *Fountain*, was a standard porcelain urinal, signed "R. Mutt." The artist was later revealed to be Marcel Duchamp. The exhibition committee, despite its promise to show every submitted work, refused to display it. Duchamp’s act was not about creating a beautiful object in the traditional sense. Instead, by selecting a mass-produced, utilitarian item and presenting it as art, he was questioning the very nature of art itself. Who decides what is art? Does an artwork have to be handmade by the artist? By choosing an object completely devoid of conventional aesthetic appeal, Duchamp “had thrown the gauntlet down” to the entire art establishment.

Vocabulary

  • scandalized충격을 받은
  • utilitarian실용적인
  • devoidof ~이 없는
  • gauntlet장갑
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 267]

The "Tragedy of the Commons" is an economic theory that describes a situation where individuals with access to a shared resource act in their own self-interest, ultimately depleting that resource. Imagine a pasture open to all local herders. Each herder rationally concludes that adding one more animal to their herd will benefit them personally, as they receive the full profit from that animal. However, the cost of the resulting overgrazing is shared by all herders. Since the individual benefit outweighs the shared cost, every herder is incentivized to add more and more animals. This continues until the pasture is destroyed, and no one can use it. The ecologist Garrett Hardin, who popularized the concept, argued that in a world of finite resources, “the logic of the commons is a remorseless engine of ruin.”

Vocabulary

  • commons공유지
  • depleting고갈시키는
  • overgrazing과도한 방목
  • remorseless무자비한
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 268]

Cognitive dissonance is the mental discomfort experienced by a person who holds two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values, or when their beliefs are inconsistent with their actions. For instance, a person who smokes cigarettes knows that smoking is harmful to their health. This creates a conflict between their behavior (smoking) and their belief (smoking is bad). To reduce this uncomfortable dissonance, the person might change their behavior (quit smoking), or, more commonly, they might change their belief or rationalize their behavior. They might downplay the health risks ("It's not as dangerous as they say") or add new cognitions ("Smoking helps me relax, which is good for my health"). This process of justification is essentially “a mental balancing act” aimed at restoring internal consistency.

Vocabulary

  • contradictory모순되는
  • dissonance부조화
  • rationalize합리화하다
  • consistency일관성
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 269]

The Panopticon, a type of institutional building designed by the philosopher Jeremy Bentham, is a powerful metaphor for modern surveillance. The design consists of a circular structure with cells around the perimeter and a central watchtower. From the tower, a single guard can observe all the inmates, but the inmates cannot see the guard. Crucially, the inmates never know whether they are being watched at any given moment. This uncertainty is the key to its power. The major effect of the Panopticon, as later analyzed by philosopher Michel Foucault, is to induce in the inmate “a state of conscious and permanent visibility” that ensures the automatic functioning of power. The inmate must behave as if they are under constant surveillance, thereby becoming their own disciplinarian.

Vocabulary

  • Panopticon판옵티콘, 원형 감옥
  • perimeter주위
  • induce유도하다
  • disciplinarian규율가
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 270]

In literary and dramatic criticism, the term "deus ex machina" refers to a plot device whereby a seemingly unsolvable problem is suddenly and abruptly resolved by an unexpected and unlikely intervention. The term is Latin for "god from the machine," originating from ancient Greek theater where actors playing gods were often lowered onto the stage by a crane-like machine to resolve a convoluted plot. While it can sometimes be used for comedic or surreal effect, it is often criticized in modern storytelling. A critic might review a novel where the heroes, trapped and facing certain doom, are suddenly saved by the coincidental arrival of a long-lost, wealthy relative. The critic would likely dismiss this ending as “a lazy deus ex machina.”

Vocabulary

  • unsolvable해결할 수 없는
  • abruptly갑자기
  • convoluted복잡한
  • coincidental우연의
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 271]

In a recent economic forum, a trade minister passionately defended the administration's new protectionist policies, such as high tariffs on imported goods. He argued that these measures were essential to protect domestic industries and jobs from foreign competition. However, a renowned economist in the audience later commented to a journalist, "The minister's strategy is a classic case of shooting oneself in the foot." He explained that while such policies might offer a temporary shield, they inevitably lead to retaliatory tariffs from other countries, increased costs for consumers, and a decline in overall economic efficiency. In the long run, the very industries the government aims to protect could be the ones that suffer the most from a disrupted global market.

Vocabulary

  • protectionist보호주의의
  • tariffs관세
  • retaliatory보복적인
  • disrupted붕괴된
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 272]

During a climate summit, an activist delivered a powerful speech criticizing the slow pace of global environmental action. She pointed to charts showing the rapid depletion of natural resources and the irreversible damage to ecosystems. She argued that many current economic models prioritize short-term profits at the expense of long-term sustainability. "When we continue this rampant exploitation of our planet," she declared, "we are not just consuming resources; we are essentially stealing from our children's piggy bank." Her statement was met with a somber silence, as it highlighted the ethical dimension of environmental degradation, framing it as a direct theft from the inheritance of the next generation.

Vocabulary

  • depletion고갈
  • rampant만연하는
  • exploitation착취
  • somber침울한
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 273]

In the midst of a heated election campaign, a charismatic populist candidate promised sweeping tax cuts for all citizens and a dramatic increase in social welfare programs, without offering a clear plan on how to fund these initiatives. His rallies drew massive, enthusiastic crowds. However, a seasoned political analyst, observing the phenomenon, wrote in her column, "The candidate's promises are a siren's song to a shipwrecked crew." She warned that while these pledges sound incredibly appealing to an electorate struggling with economic hardship, they could lure the nation into a fiscal disaster, much like the mythical sirens whose beautiful songs lured sailors to their doom on the rocks.

Vocabulary

  • populist대중영합주의자
  • initiatives계획
  • shipwrecked난파된
  • fiscal재정의

[지문 274]

An educational reformer, Dr. Evelyn Reed, strongly criticized the prevailing method of rote memorization in schools. She argued that forcing students to passively absorb vast amounts of information without understanding the underlying concepts or their real-world applications is counterproductive. "This approach," she stated at a conference, "is not true learning. It is merely building a house of cards." Dr. Reed insisted that such an education creates a fragile knowledge base that collapses under the slightest pressure of critical questioning or complex problem-solving. She advocated for a shift towards inquiry-based learning, where students actively construct their own understanding.

Vocabulary

  • reformer개혁가
  • rotememorization 기계적 암기
  • counterproductive역효과를 낳는
  • inquiry-based탐구 기반의

[지문 275]

A minor local dispute over a new community center's location quickly escalated after a sensationalist online news outlet began its coverage. The outlet published articles with inflammatory headlines, selectively quoted the most extreme opinions from both sides, and featured emotionally charged images. A veteran journalist, dismayed by the coverage, commented on the situation. "Responsible journalism should aim to inform and mediate," he said. "But what this outlet is doing is simply throwing gasoline on a small fire." He lamented that instead of fostering understanding, the media outlet was actively intensifying the conflict for the sake of generating more clicks and online traffic, turning a manageable disagreement into a major public feud.

Vocabulary

  • escalated악화되었다
  • sensationalist선정적인
  • inflammatory선동적인
  • feud불화

[지문 276]

In her latest book, sociologist Dr. Anya Sharma examines the psychological effects of modern social media. She observes that many individuals, especially the young, invest enormous amounts of time and energy curating a perfect online persona. They anxiously await likes and positive comments as validation of their self-worth. Dr. Sharma argues that this relentless pursuit is ultimately unfulfilling. She quotes one interviewee, a former influencer, who described his past life as "a constant race, chasing digital ghosts." This phrase, she explains, perfectly captures the feeling of pursuing something intangible and ultimately unreal—an idealized image and the fleeting approval of a faceless online crowd—which can never provide true, lasting happiness.

Vocabulary

  • persona페르소나, 모습
  • validation확인, 인정
  • unfulfilling만족스럽지 못한

A young, innovative software developer recently joined a large, well-established corporation known for its long history and market dominance. After a few months, he shared his frustrations with a mentor. "I have so many ideas for streamlining our processes and developing new products," he said, "but every proposal gets stuck in endless layers of bureaucracy. The management structure here is like a beautifully preserved fossil." He explained that while the company's structure was impressive for its history and stability, it was utterly rigid and incapable of adapting to the fast-paced changes of the modern tech industry. It was an artifact of a bygone era, admired but not alive.

Vocabulary

  • bureaucracy관료주의

Following tense negotiations over a disputed border territory, a high-ranking diplomat from one of the involved nations gave a press conference. When asked if his country would use military force if the other nation's troops crossed a certain river, the diplomat responded calmly, "We have made our position unequivocally clear. We consider that river to be a critical line." A political journalist covering the event later wrote that the diplomat, despite his calm demeanor, had effectively drawn a line in the sand. The journalist interpreted the statement not as a vague warning, but as a firm, non-negotiable ultimatum, signaling that any such crossing would trigger a severe and predetermined response.

  • unequivocally명백하게

An emerging painter, known for her unique and challenging style, was being interviewed about her refusal to sign with a major commercial gallery. The gallery had promised her financial success but demanded that she adopt a more mainstream, marketable style. The painter explained her decision by stating, "An artist's voice must be their own. I appreciate the offer, but I refuse to paint with someone else's brush." Through this statement, she asserted her commitment to artistic integrity over commercial gain. She believed that compromising her style would be equivalent to abandoning her identity as an artist, creating work that was not genuinely hers.

  • emerging신흥의
  • mainstream주류의
  • integrity진실성, 온전함

A historian was asked to comment on a recent trend of historical revisionism, where certain groups attempt to downplay or deny past atrocities committed by their nation. The historian expressed deep concern, arguing that a nation must confront its entire history, including its darkest chapters, to truly move forward. He said, "These attempts to sanitize the past are not only dishonest but also futile. It's like trying to change the direction of a river by moving a few stones." He elaborated that the fundamental course of historical truth is too powerful and well-documented to be altered by such minor and superficial efforts. True reconciliation, he concluded, comes from acceptance, not denial.

Vocabulary

  • atrocities잔학 행위

In the competitive world of tech startups, the story of Innovatech serves as a cautionary tale. Founded by a brilliant but overly ambitious CEO, the company saw meteoric success with its first product. However, instead of consolidating its position, the CEO, fueled by early praise and venture capital, rapidly expanded into a dozen unrelated markets. He ignored the advice of seasoned board members who warned that the company's resources were stretched too thin. Within two years, the once-promising company was facing bankruptcy, a victim of its own unchecked growth. The CEO had brilliant ideas, but in his youthful exuberance, he flew too close to the sun.

The city council was debating a proposal from a large corporation to fund a new public park. The offer seemed generous: a beautifully designed green space with modern amenities, all at no cost to the taxpayer. However, Councilwoman Eva Rostova was skeptical. She pointed out that the contract included clauses giving the corporation exclusive rights to host large, profitable events in the park and control all vending. "This proposal," she argued, "is a beautiful gift box, but I fear that once we open it, we will find it is a veritable Trojan Horse that will commercialize our public spaces for private gain."

He spends his weekends writing policy papers and his weekdays lobbying politicians, often with little to no tangible result. His friends, many of whom are successful in conventional careers, admire his passion but worry about his prospects. "We love his idealism," one friend commented, "but sometimes we feel he is tilting at windmills. The system is a behemoth, and he is just one man." They believe his efforts, however noble, are ultimately directed at an unbeatable and perhaps imaginary foe.

However, Ms. Sharma saw a spark of curiosity in his eyes. She began giving him extra attention, praising his small achievements and challenging him with tasks she knew he could handle. She told him she believed he was one of the brightest students she had ever met. Slowly, Leo's behavior and grades began to improve dramatically. By the end of the year, he was at the top of his class. Leo wasn't just a student to her; he was her own Pygmalion project.

In the Aesopian fable, the tortoise's victory is a simple lesson in perseverance. However, in today's hyper-competitive corporate environment, the story has a new layer of meaning. We see brilliant, fast-moving executives—the hares—who sprint from project to project, fueled by caffeine and ambition. They often achieve quick, spectacular results but burn out just as quickly. Their slower, more methodical colleagues—the tortoises—may not produce flashy wins, but they build sustainable systems and maintain their well-being. When a major crisis hits, it is often the tortoises who are left to pick up the pieces after the hare has taken an untimely nap.

Vocabulary

  • perseverance인내
  • methodical체계적인
  • flashy화려한

Titan Corp was an undisputed giant in the global logistics industry. Its vast network of ships, planes, and trucks was unparalleled, and its advanced tracking technology gave it a significant edge over competitors. The company seemed invincible, consistently posting record profits year after year. However, industry analysts noted that nearly 80% of Titan's highly specialized computer chips were sourced from a single supplier in a geopolitically unstable region. This extreme dependency, though cost-effective, was widely seen as the company's Achilles' heel, a vulnerability that could bring the entire empire crumbling down if the supply chain was ever disrupted.

For months, Governor Miller had been publicly noncommittal about the controversial environmental bill. It was a deeply divisive issue, pitting powerful industrial lobbyists against a passionate coalition of environmental activists. His political advisors warned him that taking a firm stance either way could alienate a crucial segment of voters in the upcoming election. However, after reviewing a new scientific report detailing the irreversible damage of current pollution levels, he called a press conference. With a somber expression, he announced his unequivocal support for the bill, stating, "The time for indecision is over." In that moment, everyone in the room knew he had crossed the Rubicon.

To apply for a research grant, the university required Professor Kim to submit Form A. However, the administration office informed him that Form A could only be processed if it was accompanied by a signed Form B. When he went to the financial department to get Form B signed, they told him that Form B could not be signed without a pre-approval code, which, they explained, was generated only upon the initial submission of Form A. He was trapped. He couldn't submit A without B, and he couldn't get B without first submitting A. Professor Kim sighed in frustration, realizing he was stuck in a bureaucratic Catch-22.

To outsiders, his life seemed like a dream. However, Richard himself felt a constant, underlying anxiety. The global economy was volatile, a hostile takeover was always a possibility, and the board of directors scrutinized his every move. The immense pressure to maintain growth and shareholder value was relentless. For Richard, the luxurious chair in his corner office felt less like a throne and more like a seat with the Sword of Damocles hanging over his head.

[지문 290]

For years, a small group of scientists had been presenting data on the alarming decline of local bee populations, warning of a potential agricultural crisis. However, their findings were often dismissed by policymakers as overly alarmist. A prominent politician, funded by industrial groups, frequently ridiculed their studies, claiming they were exaggerating the threat for research funding. When a sudden, catastrophic collapse of the bee population led to widespread crop failure, the public was caught completely off guard. The politician's constant dismissals had made many people ignore the warnings. He had, in effect, risked becoming the boy who cried wolf, but the wolf turned out to be real.

Vocabulary

  • dismissed묵살된
  • alarmist기우가 심한
  • ridiculed조롱했다
  • offguard 방심한
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 291]

The thought experiment of Schrödinger's cat, devised by physicist Erwin Schrödinger, presents a paradox that challenges our understanding of quantum mechanics and reality itself. In this scenario, a cat is placed in a sealed box with a radioactive atom that has a 50% chance of decaying within an hour, triggering a mechanism that would kill the cat. According to quantum mechanics, until the box is opened and the system is observed, the atom is in a superposition—both decayed and not decayed simultaneously. Consequently, the cat, whose fate is linked to the atom, is also in a superposition of being both alive and dead. Until the moment of observation collapses the wave function into a single definite state, the cat remains a ghost of possibilities. This unsettling idea forces us to question the nature of reality and the role of the observer in shaping it.

Vocabulary

  • devised고안된
  • superposition중첩
  • collapses붕괴시키다
  • unsettling불안하게 하는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 292]

Jeremy Bentham's Panopticon is an architectural design for a prison that has profound implications for understanding modern power structures. The design features a central observation tower surrounded by a ring of cells. The guards in the tower can see every inmate, but the inmates cannot see the guards. This asymmetry of vision creates a powerful psychological effect: because the inmates never know if they are being watched at any given moment, they are compelled to behave as if they are always under surveillance. This constant sense of visibility ensures their discipline and control. Michel Foucault, a French philosopher, argued that this mechanism of power is not just for prisons; it extends to schools, hospitals, and workplaces. In this system, external force is replaced by internalized self-regulation, meaning the inmate becomes his own jailer.

Vocabulary

  • Panopticon판옵티콘, 원형 감옥
  • asymmetry비대칭
  • compelled강요받는
  • internalized내면화된
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 293]

The Prisoner's Dilemma is a classic thought experiment in game theory that illustrates why two rational individuals might not cooperate, even when it appears that it is in their best interest to do so. Imagine two criminals arrested for a crime and held in separate cells with no means of communicating. The prosecutors lack sufficient evidence for a major conviction unless one or both confess. If both remain silent, they each serve a minor one-year sentence. If one confesses and the other remains silent, the confessor goes free while the silent one gets a ten-year sentence. If both confess, they each receive a five-year sentence. From an individual's perspective, confessing is always the safer bet, regardless of the other's choice. As a result, both are likely to confess, leading to a five-year sentence for each, a much worse outcome than the one-year sentence they would have received by cooperating. Thus, they are trapped in a sub-optimal equilibrium.

Vocabulary

  • illustrates설명하다
  • conviction유죄 판결
  • confess자백하다
  • sub-optimal차선의
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 294]

In Greek mythology, Sisyphus was a king condemned by the gods to an eternity of futile labor. His punishment was to push a colossal boulder up a hill, only to watch it roll back down each time he neared the top. He would then have to walk down to the bottom and start all over again. This endless, repetitive cycle of meaningless toil is a powerful symbol. The philosopher Albert Camus used this myth to explore the human condition in an absurd world, one devoid of inherent meaning or divine purpose. Camus argues that we, like Sisyphus, often engage in repetitive and seemingly pointless tasks. However, he suggests that acknowledging the absurdity of our existence is the first step toward freedom. By consciously accepting his fate and finding value in the struggle itself, Sisyphus becomes the master of his own rock.

Vocabulary

  • condemned선고받은
  • futile헛된
  • colossal거대한
  • absurdity부조리
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 295]

The biblical story of the Tower of Babel describes a united humanity, speaking a single language, who attempt to build a tower tall enough to reach the heavens. Seeing this as an act of hubris, God confounds their speech, creating a multitude of languages so they can no longer understand one another. Their project collapses, and humanity is scattered across the face of the Earth. This narrative is often interpreted as a cautionary tale about human arrogance. However, it also serves as a powerful metaphor for the challenges of communication in a diverse world. Even when we technically speak the same language, our different backgrounds, cultures, and experiences can create invisible barriers. In our modern, globalized society, filled with specialized jargons and cultural nuances, we are often building our own Towers of Babel.

Vocabulary

  • hubris오만
  • confounds혼란시키다
  • multitude다수
  • jargons전문 용어
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 296]

Plato's Allegory of the Cave is a cornerstone of Western philosophy. It asks us to imagine prisoners chained in a cave, facing a blank wall. Behind them, a fire burns, and puppeteers cast shadows on the wall. The prisoners, having known nothing else, mistake these shadows for reality. If one prisoner were freed and forced into the sunlight, he would be blinded by the glare and pained by the truth. He might even wish to return to the familiar darkness. However, if he persevered, his eyes would adjust, and he would come to see the true forms of objects and understand the sun as the source of all light and truth. Plato uses this allegory to argue that what we perceive as reality through our senses is merely a pale imitation of the higher, true reality of Forms. For many, the journey out of the cave is daunting, as the sunlight of truth can be a painful revelation.

Vocabulary

  • allegory풍유, 비유
  • puppeteers인형 조종자
  • persevere인내하다
  • daunting벅찬
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 297]

The Ship of Theseus is a classic philosophical paradox concerning identity over time. It raises the question: if an object has had all of its components replaced, does it remain the same object? The thought experiment imagines the famous ship of the hero Theseus. Over the years, as its wooden planks begin to rot, they are replaced one by one with new ones. Eventually, no original planks remain. Is this fully restored ship still the Ship of Theseus? Furthermore, if someone collected all the old, discarded planks and reassembled them, which of the two ships is the true Ship of Theseus? This puzzle forces us to consider what constitutes identity—is it the physical material, the form, or the history? In our own lives, as our cells replace themselves and our beliefs evolve, we are all, in a sense, vessels of Theseus.

Vocabulary

  • paradox역설
  • components구성 요소
  • discarded버려진
  • constitutes구성하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 298]

In the philosophy of science, a guiding principle known as Ockham's Razor suggests that when presented with competing hypotheses about the same prediction, one should select the one that makes the fewest assumptions. This principle is not an irrefutable law but a heuristic, a rule of thumb that prioritizes simplicity. For instance, if your car won't start, you could theorize that a sophisticated international conspiracy has sabotaged your engine, or you could assume the battery is dead. The latter explanation requires far fewer new assumptions. While the more complex theory isn't impossible, the simpler one is more likely to be true and is certainly easier to test. In the face of overly elaborate explanations for common phenomena, one might be tempted to ask, "Why multiply entities beyond necessity?"

Vocabulary

  • competing경쟁하는
  • irrefutable반박할 수 없는
  • heuristic발견적 방법
  • elaborate정교한
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 299]

John Rawls, a prominent political philosopher, introduced a thought experiment called the "Veil of Ignorance" to determine the principles of a just society. He asks us to imagine ourselves in an "original position," where we must decide on the basic structure of our society. The crucial catch is that we are behind a veil of ignorance: we do not know our own social status, class, race, gender, talents, or beliefs. We are unaware of our personal advantages or disadvantages. From this impartial standpoint, Rawls argues, any rational person would choose principles that protect the least advantaged members of society. Because you might end up in the lowest position, you would want to ensure that this position is as tolerable as possible. In this way, the veil forces our hand toward fairness.

Vocabulary

  • prominent저명한
  • impartial공정한
  • standpoint관점
  • tolerable견딜 수 있는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 300]

The concept of the butterfly effect, central to chaos theory, posits that a small change in the initial conditions of a complex system can result in large, unpredictable differences in a later state. The name comes from an analogy where the flap of a butterfly's wings in Brazil could, in theory, set off a tornado in Texas. This is not to say the butterfly *causes* the tornado directly, but that its tiny input into the system can be amplified over time, leading to a dramatically different outcome than if the butterfly had not flapped its wings. This principle challenges the deterministic view of the world, suggesting that long-term prediction is often impossible in systems like weather or economics. It reveals that in a web of immense complexity, a whisper can become a storm.

Vocabulary

  • posits상정하다
  • analogy비유
  • amplified증폭된
  • deterministic결정론적인
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 301]

The Byzantine Empire possessed a formidable secret weapon known as Greek fire, an incendiary substance that played a crucial role in its military history. ( ① ) The exact formula for this chemical weapon was a closely guarded state secret, and its composition remains a subject of scholarly debate. ( ② ) What is known is that it could be projected from siphons mounted on ships and would continue to burn even on water. ( ③ ) This made it an exceptionally effective naval weapon, giving the Byzantine fleet a significant advantage over its enemies. ( ④ ) The terrifying nature of the weapon also had a profound psychological impact on opposing forces, who often broke rank and fled at its sight. ( ⑤ ) The loss of the formula for Greek fire sometime after the 12th century marked a significant decline in the military power of the empire.

Vocabulary

  • formidable가공할, 어마어마한
  • incendiary소이의, 불을 내는
  • siphons사이펀, 관
  • repelling격퇴하는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 302]

Social mobility, the movement of individuals or groups in social position over time, is a key concept in sociology. It can be measured in terms of changes in income, occupation, or social class. ( ① ) While societies vary in their degree of openness, most modern nations provide pathways for upward mobility. ( ② ) Several factors are known to influence an individual's chances of moving up the social ladder, including family background and economic conditions. ( ③ ) However, among the most powerful engines of social mobility is education. ( ④ ) This is because education equips individuals with the necessary skills, knowledge, and credentials to compete for better-paying and more prestigious jobs. ( ⑤ ) Therefore, policies aimed at improving educational equity are often seen as a primary tool for fostering a more mobile and just society.

Vocabulary

  • mobility이동성
  • forgone포기된
  • credentials자격증
  • fostering조성하는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 303]

The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, also known as linguistic relativity, posits that the structure of a language affects its speakers' worldview or cognition. ( ① ) This theory suggests that the language we speak not only voices our thoughts but also shapes them. ( ② ) There are two versions of the hypothesis: a "strong" version, which claims language determines thought, and a "weak" version, which argues that language merely influences it. ( ③ ) Most modern linguists support the weaker version, acknowledging that language can guide and predispose us to certain ways of thinking. ( ④ ) For instance, the Pirahã people of the Amazon rainforest have a language that lacks specific words for numbers, instead using general terms like "few" and "many." ( ⑤ ) Such examples suggest a compelling link between the words we use and how we interpret the world around us.

Vocabulary

  • posits상정하다
  • predispose~하게 만드는 경향이 있는
  • approximate대략적인
  • compelling설득력 있는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 304]

Cubism, one of the most influential art movements of the early 20th century, represented a radical departure from traditional European painting. Pioneered by Pablo Picasso and Georges Braque, it challenged the long-held convention that art should imitate nature. ( ① ) Instead of depicting objects from a single, fixed viewpoint, Cubist painters portrayed their subjects from a multitude of viewpoints simultaneously. ( ② ) This technique resulted in fragmented, geometric forms that presented a more complex and conceptual reality. ( ③ ) The movement sought to represent the essence of an object by capturing it in its entirety, over time, within a single canvas. ( ④ ) Picasso's 1907 painting *Les Demoiselles d'Avignon*, for example, is considered a seminal work that shattered traditional notions of perspective and form. ( ⑤ ) This revolutionary approach paved the way for many subsequent abstract art movements.

Vocabulary

  • departure벗어남, 이탈
  • multitude다수
  • seminal중대한
  • subsequent그 후의
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 305]

The philosopher John Locke, a key figure in empiricism, proposed the concept of *tabula rasa*, or "blank slate." According to this theory, the human mind is born without any innate ideas or knowledge. ( ① ) Instead, all knowledge is gradually acquired through sensory experience and reflection. ( ② ) Locke argued that we gather simple ideas from our senses—such as "yellow," "cold," or "soft"—and then combine them in our minds to form more complex ideas, like "a cold, yellow lemon." ( ③ ) This perspective directly challenged the rationalist view, championed by philosophers like Descartes, that certain principles are inherent in the human mind from birth. ( ④ ) To illustrate, a child who is bitten by a dog develops a fear of dogs not from an innate idea of fear, but from the direct negative experience. ( ⑤ ) Thus, for Locke, experience is the ultimate source of all human knowledge and character.

Vocabulary

  • empiricism경험주의
  • innate타고난
  • inherent내재된
  • ultimate궁극적인
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 306]

In evolutionary biology, Batesian mimicry is a form of defensive adaptation in which a harmless species evolves to imitate the warning signals of a harmful species. The purpose of this mimicry is to deceive predators into believing that the mimic is also dangerous or unpalatable. ( ① ) This strategy is only successful if the predator has had a negative encounter with the genuinely harmful species, known as the model. ( ② ) Once the predator learns to avoid the model, it will also tend to avoid the mimic. ( ③ ) For instance, the harmless viceroy butterfly has evolved to mimic the distinct orange and black wing patterns of the toxic monarch butterfly. ( ④ ) The effectiveness of this mimicry depends on the population ratio; the mimic must be less common than the model for the predator's learned avoidance to remain strong. ( ⑤ ) If mimics become too numerous, predators may learn that many of the warning-colored insects are actually safe to eat.

Vocabulary

  • mimicry의태, 모방
  • unpalatable맛없는
  • viceroy부왕나비
  • monarch제왕나비
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 307]

A fundamental principle in economics is the concept of opportunity cost, which is the value of the next-best alternative that must be forgone to pursue a certain action. It is a key aspect of decision-making because resources like time, money, and effort are always limited. ( ① ) Every choice we make involves a trade-off. ( ② ) The opportunity cost is not just the monetary price of a choice but includes all the benefits you miss out on by not choosing the alternative. ( ③ ) To illustrate, if you spend three hours and $50 attending a concert, you cannot spend that time studying for an exam or that money on a new book. ( ④ ) Understanding this concept helps individuals and businesses make more rational and efficient choices by explicitly considering what they are giving up. ( ⑤ ) It forces a clear-eyed evaluation of what is truly most valuable in any given situation.

Vocabulary

  • forgone포기된
  • trade-off상충 관계
  • explicitly명시적으로
  • clear-eyed명철한
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 308]

Cognitive dissonance is a psychological theory that refers to the mental stress or discomfort experienced by an individual who holds two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values, or when their beliefs are inconsistent with their actions. ( ① ) This theory, proposed by Leon Festinger, suggests that people have a motivational drive to reduce this dissonance by changing their attitudes, beliefs, or behaviors. ( ② ) The discomfort is often so strong that it compels individuals to seek consistency. ( ③ ) For example, a person who considers themselves environmentally conscious but drives a gas-guzzling SUV experiences this form of mental discomfort. ( ④ ) These resolutions are all attempts to restore balance and reduce the psychological tension. ( ⑤ ) Understanding cognitive dissonance provides insight into why people sometimes make seemingly irrational decisions or change their minds unexpectedly.

Vocabulary

  • contradictory모순되는
  • dissonance부조화
  • rationalize합리화하다
  • negligible무시할 만한
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 309]

A trophic cascade is an ecological phenomenon triggered by the addition or removal of top predators, which results in dramatic changes in the ecosystem structure and nutrient cycling. These powerful indirect interactions can control entire ecosystems. ( ① ) When a top predator is removed, the population of its primary prey can explode. ( ② ) This, in turn, can lead to the overconsumption of primary producers like plants, fundamentally altering the landscape. ( ③ ) For instance, the reintroduction of wolves into Yellowstone National Park in 1995 triggered a cascade of ecological changes. ( ④ ) The wolves preyed on elk, causing a decrease in the elk population and changing their grazing behavior. ( ⑤ ) This example powerfully demonstrates how a single keystone species can shape the health and structure of its entire environment.

Vocabulary

  • trophiccascade 영양 폭포
  • predators포식자
  • prey먹이
  • vegetation초목
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 310]

The invention of the printing press by Johannes Gutenberg in the mid-15th century was a pivotal event in world history, fundamentally altering the way information was created and disseminated. Before the press, books were rare and expensive, copied by hand primarily by monks. ( ① ) This process was slow, laborious, and prone to errors, severely limiting the spread of knowledge to a small, elite circle. ( ② ) The printing press, however, allowed for the mass production of written materials quickly and cheaply. ( ③ ) This sudden accessibility of information empowered new ideas to spread with unprecedented speed, challenging established authorities like the Church and monarchies. ( ④ ) For instance, Martin Luther's Ninety-five Theses, which sparked the Protestant Reformation, were printed and distributed widely across Germany within weeks. ( ⑤ ) Consequently, the printing press is often credited with fueling the Renaissance, the Reformation, and the Age of Enlightenment.

Vocabulary

  • pivotal중심축이 되는
  • disseminated보급된
  • laborious힘든
  • advent출현
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 311]

The Earth's climate system is characterized by complex feedback loops, where an initial change can trigger a series of subsequent changes that either amplify or dampen the original effect. One of the most critical examples of an amplifying feedback loop involves the melting of Arctic sea ice. ( ① ) As global temperatures rise, the white, reflective sea ice begins to melt, exposing the darker ocean water beneath it. ( ② ) This darker surface has a lower albedo, meaning it absorbs more of the sun's energy rather than reflecting it back into space. ( ③ ) The increased absorption of solar radiation leads to further warming of the ocean, which in turn causes even more sea ice to melt. ( ④ ) This cycle creates a self-reinforcing process that significantly accelerates the rate of warming in the Arctic region, with global consequences. ( ⑤ ) Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for accurately predicting future climate scenarios.

Vocabulary

  • feedbackloops 피드백 루프
  • amplify증폭시키다
  • albedo알베도, 반사율
  • self-reinforcing자기 강화적인
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 312]

In macroeconomics, the relationship between inflation and unemployment has long been a subject of debate. The traditional view, first observed by A.W. Phillips, suggested that policymakers faced a trade-off between these two economic indicators. ( ① ) According to this theory, policies designed to lower unemployment, such as increasing government spending or cutting taxes, would tend to increase the demand for goods and services. ( ② ) This heightened demand would, in turn, lead to higher wages and prices, resulting in inflation. ( ③ ) Conversely, measures aimed at curbing inflation, like raising interest rates, would likely slow down economic activity and lead to higher unemployment. ( ④ ) This perspective dominated economic policy for decades, suggesting that a government could not simultaneously achieve both low inflation and low unemployment. ( ⑤ ) However, later economic events, such as the stagflation of the 1970s, challenged this simple trade-off.

Vocabulary

  • trade-off상충 관계
  • heightened고조된
  • curbing억제하는
  • stagflation스태그플레이션
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 313]

The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, also known as linguistic relativity, posits that the structure of a language affects its speakers' worldview or cognition. ( ① ) This theory suggests that the language we speak not only voices our thoughts but also shapes them. ( ② ) There are two versions of the hypothesis: a "strong" version, which claims language determines thought, and a "weak" version, which argues that language merely influences it. ( ③ ) Most modern linguists support the weaker version, acknowledging that language can guide and predispose us to certain ways of thinking. ( ④ ) This linguistic feature is believed to be connected to a cognitive trait where they perceive quantities in an approximate rather than exact manner. ( ⑤ ) Such examples suggest a compelling link between the words we use and how we interpret the world around us.

Vocabulary

  • posits상정하다
  • predispose~하게 만드는 경향이 있는
  • approximate대략적인
  • compelling설득력 있는

( ① ) Instead of depicting objects from a single, fixed viewpoint, Cubist painters portrayed their subjects from a multitude of viewpoints simultaneously. ( ② ) This technique resulted in fragmented, geometric forms that presented a more complex and conceptual reality. ( ③ ) The movement sought to represent the essence of an object by capturing it in its entirety, over time, within a single canvas. ( ④ ) In it, the figures are rendered as sharp, angular planes, defying the rounded and idealized forms of the past. ( ⑤ ) This revolutionary approach paved the way for many subsequent abstract art movements.

  • laborious힘든

Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 321]

The Earth's climate system is characterized by complex feedback loops, where an initial change can trigger a series of subsequent changes that either amplify or dampen the original effect. One of the most critical examples of an amplifying feedback loop involves the melting of Arctic sea ice. ( ① ) As global temperatures rise, the white, reflective sea ice begins to melt, exposing the darker ocean water beneath it. ( ② ) This darker surface has a lower albedo, meaning it absorbs more of the sun's energy rather than reflecting it back into space. ( ③ ) The increased absorption of solar radiation leads to further warming of the ocean, which in turn causes even more sea ice to melt. ( ④ ) This phenomenon, known as the ice-albedo feedback, is a powerful amplifier of climate change. ( ⑤ ) Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for accurately predicting future climate scenarios.

Vocabulary

  • feedbackloops 피드백 루프
  • amplify증폭시키다
  • albedo알베도, 반사율
  • self-reinforcing자기 강화적인
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 322]

In macroeconomics, the relationship between inflation and unemployment has long been a subject of debate. The traditional view, first observed by A.W. Phillips, suggested that policymakers faced a trade-off between these two economic indicators. ( ① ) In essence, there was believed to be a stable and inverse relationship between the rate of unemployment and the rate of inflation. ( ② ) This heightened demand would, in turn, lead to higher wages and prices, resulting in inflation. ( ③ ) Conversely, measures aimed at curbing inflation, like raising interest rates, would likely slow down economic activity and lead to higher unemployment. ( ④ ) This perspective dominated economic policy for decades, suggesting that a government could not simultaneously achieve both low inflation and low unemployment. ( ⑤ ) However, later economic events, such as the stagflation of the 1970s, challenged this simple trade-off.

Vocabulary

  • trade-off상충 관계
  • heightened고조된
  • curbing억제하는
  • stagflation스태그플레이션
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 323]

The theory of cognitive dissonance, proposed by Leon Festinger, explores the mental discomfort experienced by a person who holds two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values, or is confronted by new information that conflicts with existing beliefs. ( ① ) For instance, a person who considers themselves environmentally conscious might purchase a gas-guzzling SUV. ( ② ) This conflict between their belief ("I am environmentally friendly") and their action (buying an inefficient car) creates a sense of internal inconsistency. ( ③ ) Such a state of psychological discomfort is what Festinger termed "cognitive dissonance." ( ④ ) They might sell the car, but more often, they will change their attitude or belief. For example, they might start to believe that one person's car choice has a negligible impact on the environment or that the SUV's safety features are more important. ( ⑤ ) This change reduces the mental discomfort and restores a sense of balance.

Vocabulary

  • contradictory모순되는
  • inconsistency비일관성
  • negligible무시할 만한
  • restores회복시키다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 324]

In the legal system, the rules of evidence are designed to ensure that trials are fair and verdicts are based on reliable information. One of the most fundamental of these is the hearsay rule, which generally prohibits the use of out-of-court statements to prove the truth of the matter asserted. ( ① ) The primary reason for this rule is that the person who originally made the statement (the declarant) is not in court, under oath, and subject to cross-examination. ( ② ) This lack of opportunity for scrutiny makes such statements inherently less reliable than direct testimony. ( ③ ) However, the law recognizes numerous exceptions to this rule where the hearsay statement is deemed to have a high degree of trustworthiness. ( ④ ) This is known as the "excited utterance" exception. ( ⑤ ) Without these carefully crafted rules, the integrity of the judicial process could be compromised.

Vocabulary

  • hearsay전문, 전해들은 말
  • prohibits금지하다
  • scrutiny정밀 조사
  • admissible인정되는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 325]

The principle of the separation of powers is a cornerstone of modern democratic governance, most famously articulated by Montesquieu. It advocates for the division of governmental authority into distinct branches, each with its own responsibilities and powers. ( ① ) Typically, these are the legislative branch (which makes laws), the executive branch (which enforces laws), and the judicial branch (which interprets laws). ( ② ) The core idea is to prevent the concentration of power in any single entity, which could lead to tyranny or abuse. ( ③ ) This division of authority is not absolute; instead, it is structured to create a system of checks and balances. ( ④ ) In turn, the legislature can override that veto, and the judiciary can declare the law unconstitutional. ( ⑤ ) This intricate system ensures that power is shared and that each branch can hold the others accountable.

Vocabulary

  • articulated명확히 표현된
  • legislative입법의
  • tyranny폭정
  • veto거부권
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 326]

Gentrification is a complex urban process whereby a neighborhood undergoes a significant transformation, often marked by an influx of wealthier residents and investment. ( ① ) It typically begins when artists, students, or other "urban pioneers" are attracted to a neglected, low-income neighborhood due to its affordable rents and unique character. ( ② ) Their presence often makes the area more fashionable and appealing. ( ③ ) This demographic shift attracts real estate developers and new businesses, who see potential for profit, further accelerating the rise in housing costs. ( ④ ) This displacement is one of the most controversial aspects of the process. ( ⑤ ) While gentrification can lead to reduced crime rates and improved infrastructure, it also raises critical questions about social equity and the right to the city.

Vocabulary

  • influx유입
  • pioneers개척자
  • escalate급등하다
  • fabric구조, 직물
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 327]

Ecosystems are intricate webs of relationships, and the removal of a single species can have unexpected and far-reaching consequences. A classic example of this is the concept of a trophic cascade, which illustrates how predators at the top of the food chain can indirectly influence the entire ecosystem structure. ( ① ) Consider the reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone National Park. ( ② ) Before their return, the elk population had grown unchecked, leading to overgrazing of willow and aspen trees, especially along rivers. ( ③ ) When wolves were reintroduced, they began to prey on the elk, causing the elk to not only decrease in number but also change their behavior to avoid certain areas. ( ④ ) This led to the recovery of riverside vegetation, which in turn stabilized riverbanks, provided habitats for birds and beavers, and even changed the course of the rivers themselves. ( ⑤ ) This cascading effect, starting from the apex predator and flowing down through the food web, demonstrates the profound impact of keystone species.

Vocabulary

  • intricate복잡한
  • trophiccascade 영양 폭포
  • unchecked억제되지 않은
  • apex정점
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 328]

The relationship between language and thought has been a central question in linguistics and psychology, most famously encapsulated in the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis. ( ① ) This hypothesis posits that the structure of a language affects its speakers' worldview or cognition. ( ② ) The "strong" version, known as linguistic determinism, argues that language entirely determines our thought processes—we can only think in the categories provided by our language. ( ③ ) This deterministic view is now largely rejected by the scientific community as it is too rigid and lacks empirical support. ( ④ ) Instead, a "weak" version of the hypothesis, called linguistic relativity, has gained more acceptance. ( ⑤ ) For example, a language with many words for different types of snow may encourage its speakers to pay closer attention to those distinctions, but it does not prevent speakers of other languages from perceiving them.

Vocabulary

  • encapsulated요약된
  • determinism결정론
  • empirical경험적인
  • nuanced미묘한 차이가 있는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 329]

The invention of the printing press by Johannes Gutenberg in the mid-15th century was a pivotal event in human history, fundamentally altering the way information was created and disseminated. ( ① ) Before its invention, books were rare and expensive, meticulously copied by hand by scribes, a process that was both time-consuming and prone to error. ( ② ) The printing press allowed for the mass production of texts at a fraction of the cost. ( ③ ) Suddenly, books and pamphlets became available not just to the clergy and the nobility, but to a growing middle class and even some common people. ( ④ ) This newfound accessibility of knowledge empowered individuals to question traditional authorities and form their own opinions. ( ⑤ ) It laid the groundwork for the modern knowledge-based society.

Vocabulary

  • pivotal중심축이 되는
  • meticulously꼼꼼하게
  • clergy성직자
  • groundwork토대
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 330]

Utilitarianism, a prominent ethical theory developed by philosophers like Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill, proposes that the best moral action is the one that maximizes utility, usually defined as that which maximizes happiness or well-being for the greatest number of people. ( ① ) This principle, often summarized as "the greatest good for the greatest number," provides a straightforward framework for making ethical decisions. ( ② ) For example, when deciding on a public policy, a utilitarian would weigh the total benefits it brings to society against the total costs and harms. ( ③ ) If the overall happiness increases, the policy is considered morally right. ( ④ ) Such a calculation, however, raises a significant ethical problem. ( ⑤ ) This challenge highlights the tension between utilitarian calculations and the concept of inalienable individual rights.

Vocabulary

  • utilitarianism공리주의
  • utility효용
  • justify정당화하다
  • inalienable양도할 수 없는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 331]

Vaccines are a cornerstone of modern medicine, working by preparing the body's immune system to fight off future infections. The process begins with the introduction of a weakened or inactivated form of a pathogen, such as a virus or bacterium, into the body. ( ① ) This harmless version of the pathogen is known as an antigen. ( ② ) Upon detecting this antigen, the immune system is triggered to mount a response, just as it would against a real infection. ( ③ ) It produces antibodies, which are specialized proteins that can neutralize the invader. ( ④ ) These cells remain in the body for a long time, sometimes for a lifetime. ( ⑤ ) If the person is later exposed to the actual, harmful pathogen, these memory cells can quickly recognize it and launch a rapid and powerful immune response, preventing the disease from developing.

Vocabulary

  • cornerstone초석
  • inactivated비활성화된
  • antigen항원
  • neutralize무력화하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 332]

Bioremediation is an innovative environmental technology that uses living organisms, primarily microorganisms, to clean up contaminated sites. This process leverages the natural metabolic pathways of microbes to break down pollutants. ( ① ) When a site, such as soil or groundwater, is contaminated with substances like oil or pesticides, specific microorganisms that can feed on these contaminants are introduced or stimulated. ( ② ) These microbes essentially "eat" the pollutants, using them as a source of food and energy. ( ③ ) This transformation is the core of the cleanup process, effectively neutralizing the threat to the ecosystem. ( ④ ) The effectiveness of bioremediation depends on various factors, including temperature, nutrient availability, and the type of contaminant. ( ⑤ ) It is considered a green solution because it relies on natural processes and minimizes the need for harsh chemicals.

Vocabulary

  • bioremediation생물학적 정화
  • leverages활용하다
  • metabolic신진대사의
  • contaminants오염물질
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 333]

Blockchain technology is most famous for powering cryptocurrencies, but its core principles have far-reaching implications. The defining feature of blockchain is its decentralized nature. ( ① ) Unlike traditional databases that are stored in a central location, a blockchain is a distributed ledger, meaning an identical copy of the transaction record is held by numerous computers across a network. ( ② ) Whenever a new transaction occurs, it is verified by the network participants and added as a new "block" to the "chain." ( ③ ) As a result, no single entity has control over the entire network, which enhances security and transparency. ( ④ ) If a malicious actor wanted to alter a transaction, they would need to alter the block on a majority of the computers in the network simultaneously, which is practically impossible. ( ⑤ ) This characteristic makes blockchain a promising technology for applications beyond finance, including supply chain management and voting systems.

Vocabulary

  • decentralized분산된
  • ledger원장
  • resilient회복력 있는
  • tampering간섭, 조작
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 334]

Rituals are a fundamental aspect of human culture, found in every known society. They are structured sequences of actions and words that are performed in a specific order. ( ① ) While the content of rituals varies widely—from religious ceremonies to national holidays—their social function often remains consistent. ( ② ) One of the primary functions is to bring people together and create a sense of community. ( ③ ) During a ritual, individuals set aside their personal concerns and focus on a common purpose or belief, acting in a synchronized manner. ( ④ ) Consequently, these shared experiences strengthen the bonds among participants and reinforce a sense of collective identity. ( ⑤ ) Therefore, rituals are not just empty traditions; they are powerful tools for building and sustaining social groups.

Vocabulary

  • rituals의식
  • consistent일관된
  • synchronized동조화된
  • cohesion응집력
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 335]

The educational psychologist Lev Vygotsky introduced the concept of the Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD) to describe the sweet spot for learning. The ZPD is the gap between what a learner can do independently and what they can achieve with guidance from a more knowledgeable person. ( ① ) This guide could be a teacher, a peer, or even a parent. ( ② ) Vygotsky's theory posits that learning is most effective when it occurs within this zone. ( ③ ) For instance, a child might not be able to solve a complex math problem alone, but they can solve it with targeted hints from a teacher. ( ④ ) As a result, the learner can successfully perform the task and internalize the new skill, effectively expanding their independent capabilities. ( ⑤ ) This process is not about simply being given the answer, but about being guided to discover it.

Vocabulary

  • proximal근접한
  • scaffold비계, 발판
  • internalize내면화하다
  • capabilities능력
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 336]

Photosynthesis is the vital process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy. This complex process can be divided into two main stages. ( ① ) The first stage is known as the light-dependent reactions, which take place in the thylakoid membranes of chloroplasts. ( ② ) During this stage, chlorophyll and other pigments absorb energy from sunlight. ( ③ ) This captured light energy is used to split water molecules, releasing oxygen as a byproduct. ( ④ ) Consequently, the energy captured from sunlight is stored in the chemical bonds of molecules like ATP and NADPH. ( ⑤ ) In the Calvin cycle, this stored chemical energy is used to convert carbon dioxide from the atmosphere into glucose, a sugar that provides energy for the plant's growth and development.

Vocabulary

  • chloroplasts엽록체
  • pigments색소
  • byproduct부산물
  • glucose포도당
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 337]

The Earth's crust is in a constant state of change, leading to the formation of three main types of rocks: igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic. Metamorphic rocks are formed from the transformation of existing rock types in a process called metamorphism. ( ① ) This process occurs when pre-existing rocks are subjected to intense heat, high pressure, or chemical reactions deep within the Earth. ( ② ) The conditions for metamorphism are typically found where tectonic plates collide or where magma intrudes into the surrounding rock. ( ③ ) As a result, the original rock's mineral composition and texture are fundamentally altered, creating a new type of rock. ( ④ ) The degree of change depends on the intensity of the heat and pressure applied. ( ⑤ ) This transformation does not melt the rock, but rather recrystallizes it, distinguishing metamorphic rocks from igneous rocks, which form from molten material.

Vocabulary

  • metamorphic변성의
  • subjectedto ~을 받는
  • intrudes침입하다
  • recrystallizes재결정하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 338]

The law of supply and demand is a fundamental concept in economics that explains how prices are determined in a market economy. It states that the price of a good will adjust to the point where the quantity demanded by consumers equals the quantity supplied by producers. ( ① ) This point is known as the equilibrium price. ( ② ) However, markets are not always in equilibrium. ( ③ ) When the quantity supplied of a good exceeds the quantity demanded at a certain price, a situation known as a surplus occurs. ( ④ ) Consequently, producers are often forced to lower their prices to sell off the excess inventory and attract more buyers. ( ⑤ ) Conversely, if demand exceeds supply, a shortage occurs, which tends to drive prices up.

Vocabulary

  • equilibrium평형, 균형
  • surplus과잉
  • inventory재고
  • shortage부족
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 339]

Classical conditioning is a learning process first demonstrated by the Russian physiologist Ivan Pavlov. His famous experiments with dogs revealed how associations between stimuli can lead to new behaviors. ( ① ) In his setup, the presentation of food (an unconditioned stimulus) naturally caused the dogs to salivate (an unconditioned response). ( ② ) Pavlov then began to ring a bell (a neutral stimulus) just before presenting the food. ( ③ ) He repeated this pairing of the bell and the food multiple times. ( ④ ) As a result, the neutral stimulus alone begins to elicit the response that was originally triggered only by the unconditioned stimulus. ( ⑤ ) This principle explains how many involuntary responses, such as fears and phobias, can be learned.

Vocabulary

  • conditioning조건 형성
  • stimulus자극
  • salivate침을 흘리다
  • elicit이끌어내다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 340]

Osmosis is a crucial biological process that involves the movement of water across a semipermeable membrane. This type of membrane allows small molecules like water to pass through but blocks larger molecules like salt or sugar (solutes). ( ① ) The direction of water movement is determined by the concentration of solutes on either side of the membrane. ( ② ) Specifically, water will always move from an area of lower solute concentration to an area of higher solute concentration. ( ③ ) This net movement of water continues until the concentrations reach a state of balance or equilibrium. ( ④ ) Consequently, the concentration of the solute becomes more equal on both sides of the membrane. ( ⑤ ) Without osmosis, cells would not be able to maintain their internal environment and would quickly perish.

Vocabulary

  • osmosis삼투
  • semipermeable반투과성의
  • solutes용질
  • perish소멸하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 341]

The formation of the Himalayas is a dramatic story of geological change, a direct result of plate tectonics. Millions of years ago, the landmass that is now the Indian subcontinent was an island moving steadily northward across the Tethys Ocean. ( ① ) This journey culminated in a colossal collision with the Eurasian Plate. ( ② ) The immense pressure from this impact had to be released. ( ③ ) In addition, because both were continental plates of similar density, neither could be subducted under the other. ( ④ ) This process is not over; the Indian Plate continues to push against the Eurasian Plate, causing the Himalayas to rise by a few millimeters each year. ( ⑤ ) This ongoing tectonic activity is also the reason for the region's frequent and powerful earthquakes.

Vocabulary

  • culminatedin ~으로 절정에 달했다
  • colossal거대한
  • subducted섭입된
  • tectonic지각 구조의
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 342]

The mid-19th century witnessed a radical transformation in naval warfare, primarily driven by technological advancements. For centuries, wooden sailing ships had dominated the seas, but their vulnerability was starkly exposed by the invention of explosive naval shells. ( ① ) These shells could easily tear through wooden hulls, rendering traditional warships obsolete. ( ② ) In response, naval engineers began cladding ships in thick iron plates, creating the first "ironclads." ( ③ ) Furthermore, the adoption of steam engines freed these new warships from the unpredictability of the wind, granting them unprecedented tactical mobility. ( ④ ) The Battle of Hampton Roads during the American Civil War famously demonstrated the superiority of ironclads over their wooden counterparts, marking a definitive end to the age of sail in military contexts. ( ⑤ ) This combination of armor and independent propulsion defined the next generation of naval power.

Vocabulary

  • navalwarfare 해전
  • obsolete구식의
  • cladding외장을 입히는
  • resilient회복력 있는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 343]

For nearly three decades after World War II, the global economy operated under the Bretton Woods system, which pegged major currencies to the U.S. dollar, itself convertible to gold at a fixed rate. This created a stable, predictable environment for international trade. ( ① ) However, by the early 1970s, economic pressures, particularly from U.S. spending on the Vietnam War and domestic programs, made the system unsustainable. ( ② ) In 1971, the United States unilaterally terminated the convertibility of the dollar to gold, effectively collapsing the Bretton Woods agreement. ( ③ ) This led to the modern era of floating exchange rates, where currency values are determined by supply and demand in the foreign exchange market. ( ④ ) In addition, this new system allowed countries to use monetary policy more freely to address domestic economic issues like unemployment or inflation. ( ⑤ )

Vocabulary

  • pegged고정된
  • unsustainable지속 불가능한
  • unilaterally일방적으로
  • volatile변동성이 큰
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 344]

The transition from medieval art to the Renaissance marked one of the most significant shifts in Western cultural history. Medieval art was predominantly religious in theme and symbolic in nature, often depicting figures in a flat, stylized manner without a strong sense of realism. ( ① ) The primary goal was to convey spiritual truths rather than to represent the natural world accurately. ( ② ) With the dawn of the Renaissance, however, a renewed interest in classical humanism led to a profound change in artistic focus. ( ③ ) Artists started to celebrate the human form, studying anatomy to portray figures with lifelike accuracy and emotional depth. ( ④ ) Furthermore, artists began to employ new techniques like linear perspective to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space on a two-dimensional surface. ( ⑤ ) This combination of humanistic subjects and realistic techniques defined the art of the era.

Vocabulary

  • predominantly주로
  • stylized양식화된
  • anatomy해부학
  • linearperspective 선형 원근법

[지문 345]

The proliferation of social network services (SNS) has fundamentally altered the dynamics of information diffusion. In traditional media, information flowed in a top-down manner, from a few sources to a large audience. ( ① ) On SNS, however, any user can be a source, leading to a more decentralized and complex pattern of dissemination. ( ② ) The virality of a piece of content often depends on its perceived novelty, emotional resonance, and the credibility of the initial sharer. ( ③ ) A message originating from a highly trusted individual is more likely to be accepted and passed on. ( ④ ) In addition, the network structure itself—who is connected to whom—plays a critical role in how widely and quickly information spreads. ( ⑤ ) Understanding these factors is crucial for anyone trying to communicate effectively in the digital age.

Vocabulary

  • proliferation확산
  • diffusion보급, 확산
  • dissemination보급
