[지문 389]

Stoicism, an ancient school of philosophy founded in Athens, offers a practical guide to living a virtuous life. ( ① ) Its central tenet is that virtue is the only true good and that external things like health, wealth, and pleasure are neither good nor bad in themselves. ( ② ) The Stoics taught that we cannot control external events, but we can control our judgments about them. ( ③ ) Therefore, the path to happiness lies in focusing on what is within our power—our thoughts, attitudes, and actions. ( ④ ) By distinguishing between what we can and cannot control, we can avoid being disturbed by misfortune or the actions of others. ( ⑤ )

Vocabulary

  • virtuous덕이 있는
  • tenet원칙
  • tranquility평온
  • misfortune불운
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 390]

Confirmation bias is the cognitive tendency to search for, interpret, favor, and recall information in a way that confirms or supports one's preexisting beliefs. ( ① ) This mental shortcut affects how we gather information and how we interpret and remember it. ( ② ) For example, a person who believes that left-handed people are more creative than right-handed people will be more likely to notice and remember instances of creative left-handers, while ignoring or forgetting counterexamples. ( ③ ) This bias is not necessarily intentional; it often operates at an unconscious level as the brain tries to simplify information processing. ( ④ ) However, it can have serious negative consequences. ( ⑤ )

Vocabulary

  • confirmationbias 확증 편향
  • preexisting기존의
  • counterexamples반례
  • compelling설득력 있는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 391]

The world of marketing can be broadly divided into two distinct categories: traditional and digital. Traditional marketing refers to any type of promotion or advertising that has been in use by companies for years, long before the internet became a household utility. ( ① ) It includes methods such as print advertisements in newspapers and magazines, broadcast commercials on television and radio, and direct mail. ( ② ) The primary characteristic of this approach is its wide, and often untargeted, reach, casting a broad net to capture as many potential customers as possible. ( ③ ) In contrast, digital marketing leverages online platforms to connect with a specific audience in a more interactive and measurable way. ( ④ ) For instance, social media marketing and search engine optimization (SEO) are powerful tools within this domain. ( ⑤ ) While both approaches aim to attract customers, they operate on fundamentally different principles and utilize different channels.

Vocabulary

  • leverages활용하다
  • demographics인구 통계
  • gauge측정하다
  • domain영역
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 392]

Democracy, a system of government by the whole population, can be implemented in different ways. The two primary forms are direct democracy and representative democracy. In a direct democracy, citizens themselves vote on laws and policies directly, rather than electing officials to make decisions on their behalf. ( ① ) This model was famously practiced in ancient Athens, where eligible citizens could speak and vote in the assembly. ( ② ) While it represents the purest form of popular rule, it can be cumbersome and inefficient, especially as the population grows. ( ③ ) On the other hand, representative democracy is often considered more practical for large, modern nations where such direct participation is unfeasible. ( ④ ) These elected officials are accountable to the people and can be replaced in subsequent elections. ( ⑤ ) Thus, the choice between these systems often involves a trade-off between direct citizen control and governmental efficiency.

Vocabulary

  • implemented시행된
  • cumbersome번거로운
  • unfeasible실행 불가능한
  • accountable책임이 있는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 393]

The transition from the Classical to the Romantic era in Western music represents a significant shift in artistic philosophy. The Classical period, spanning roughly from 1750 to 1820, is characterized by its emphasis on clarity, balance, and structural order. ( ① ) Composers like Haydn and Mozart are prime examples, creating works that are elegant, restrained, and formally precise. ( ② ) Their music often follows predictable patterns and structures, such as the sonata form, aiming for universal beauty rather than personal confession. ( ③ ) Conversely, the Romantic era that followed prioritized emotional expression and individual subjectivity above all else. ( ④ ) Composers such as Beethoven, Schubert, and Chopin broke free from the rigid constraints of the earlier period, exploring new harmonies, dynamic ranges, and longer, more complex forms. ( ⑤ ) This fundamental change in focus redefined the purpose and potential of music for generations to come.

Vocabulary

  • spanning~에 걸친
  • restrained절제된
  • subjectivity주관성
  • constraints제약
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 394]

Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR) are two transformative technologies that are often confused, but they offer fundamentally different experiences. VR aims to create a completely immersive, artificial world that replaces the user's real-world surroundings. ( ① ) Users typically wear a headset that blocks out external stimuli, transporting them to a computer-generated environment, be it a fantasy landscape or a simulated training ground. ( ② ) This total immersion is the defining feature of the VR experience. ( ③ ) Augmented Reality (AR), however, operates on a different principle by enhancing the user's existing environment rather than replacing it. ( ④ ) A well-known example is the mobile game Pokémon GO, where players see digital creatures in their actual physical surroundings through their phone's camera. ( ⑤ ) In essence, VR creates a new reality, while AR alters the one we already perceive.

Vocabulary

  • immersive몰입형의
  • stimuli자극(복수형)
  • overlays겹쳐 놓다
  • alters바꾸다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 395]

Symbiosis describes a close and long-term interaction between two different biological species. These relationships can be categorized based on the outcome for each participant. ( ① ) One of the most well-known types is mutualism, in which both species benefit from the interaction. ( ② ) For example, bees get nectar from flowers for food, and in the process, they pollinate the flowers, allowing them to reproduce. ( ③ ) A starkly different form of symbiosis is parasitism, where the relationship is not mutually beneficial. ( ④ ) A classic example is a tick feeding on the blood of a dog; the tick gains nourishment while the dog suffers from blood loss and potential disease transmission. ( ⑤ ) Understanding these different symbiotic relationships is crucial for comprehending the complex web of life in any ecosystem.

Vocabulary

  • symbiosis공생
  • mutualism상리공생
  • parasitism기생
  • atthe expense of ~을 희생하여
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 396]

In academic and scientific fields, research methodologies are often categorized as either quantitative or qualitative. Quantitative research is concerned with collecting and analyzing numerical data to identify patterns, make predictions, and test causal relationships. ( ① ) It typically involves large sample sizes and statistical analysis to ensure that the findings are generalizable to a larger population. ( ② ) The goal is often to quantify a problem and understand its prevalence by seeking objective, measurable evidence. ( ③ ) Qualitative research, in contrast, focuses on understanding the 'why' and 'how' through non-numerical data like interviews and observations. ( ④ ) Instead of large samples, it often uses smaller, more focused samples to explore topics in rich detail. ( ⑤ ) Both methods have their own strengths and are often used in combination to provide a more comprehensive understanding of a research problem.

Vocabulary

  • quantitative양적인
  • qualitative질적인
  • generalizable일반화할 수 있는
  • comprehensive포괄적인
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 397]

The concept of a mindset, popularized by psychologist Carol Dweck, profoundly influences how we approach challenges and learning. A person with a fixed mindset believes that their basic qualities, like intelligence or talent, are simply fixed traits. ( ① ) They assume they have a certain amount of ability and that's that, which leads them to avoid challenges for fear of appearing less capable. ( ② ) When faced with a setback, they may easily become discouraged or defensive, viewing failure as a definitive statement of their limitations. ( ③ ) Conversely, individuals with a growth mindset believe that their talents can be developed through hard work, good strategies, and input from others. ( ④ ) They see failure not as a lack of ability but as a valuable opportunity to grow and improve their existing skills. ( ⑤ ) Therefore, cultivating a growth mindset is a key factor in achieving personal and professional success.

Vocabulary

  • mindset사고방식
  • fixedtraits 고정된 특성
  • setback좌절
  • resilience회복탄력성
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 398]

Energy sources are fundamentally classified into two types: non-renewable and renewable. Non-renewable energy comes from sources that will run out or will not be replenished in our lifetimes—or even in many, many lifetimes. ( ① ) Most non-renewable energy sources are fossil fuels, such as coal, petroleum, and natural gas. ( ② ) The burning of these fuels is a primary contributor to air pollution and climate change, posing significant environmental risks. ( ③ ) Renewable energy sources, on the other hand, are replenished naturally and are considered environmentally sustainable. ( ④ ) For example, solar panels convert sunlight into electricity without emitting harmful greenhouse gases. ( ⑤ ) The transition from non-renewable to renewable energy is one of the most critical challenges of the 21st century.

Vocabulary

  • non-renewable비재생의
  • replenished보충된
  • contributor기여자, 원인
  • geothermal지열의
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 399]

Among the Hellenistic philosophies, Stoicism and Epicureanism both offered frameworks for achieving a good life, though their approaches differed significantly. Stoicism taught that virtue, the highest good, is based on knowledge and that the wise live in harmony with divine Reason that governs nature. ( ① ) The Stoics aimed for a state of *ataraxia*, or freedom from passion, by accepting events as they come without emotional disturbance. ( ② ) This state of tranquility was the ultimate goal for its followers. ( ③ ) Similarly, Epicureanism also sought a tranquil life, though it defined the path to it through the pursuit of modest pleasures and the avoidance of pain. ( ④ ) While a Stoic finds peace through rational acceptance of fate, an Epicurean finds it by carefully managing desires. ( ⑤ ) Despite their differences, both philosophies provided enduring guidance on how to live a meaningful and untroubled life.

Vocabulary

  • Hellenistic헬레니즘의
  • ataraxia아타락시아, 마음의 평정
  • contemplation사색
  • enduring지속적인
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 400]

Speciation, the process by which new biological species arise, can occur through different mechanisms. One major mode is allopatric speciation, which involves a geographic barrier. ( ① ) This happens when a population is divided by a physical obstacle, such as a mountain range, a river, or a glacier, preventing gene flow between the separated groups. ( ② ) Over time, these isolated populations evolve independently, accumulating genetic differences due to mutation, natural selection, and genetic drift, until they can no longer interbreed. ( ③ ) In contrast, sympatric speciation occurs when new species evolve from a single ancestral species while inhabiting the same geographic region. ( ④ ) In this scenario, there is no physical barrier separating the populations. ( ⑤ ) Both mechanisms demonstrate how the diversity of life on Earth can emerge from a common ancestry.

Vocabulary

  • speciation종분화
  • allopatric이소적
  • sympatric동소적
  • polyploidy다배수성
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 401]

It is a common social belief that creativity is the domain of lone geniuses, individuals who, through some innate and mysterious talent, produce groundbreaking works of art or science. This "lone genius" myth romanticizes the creative process, portraying it as a flash of inspiration that strikes an isolated individual. (A) This perspective suggests that what we often label as individual genius is, in fact, the outcome of a person being in the right network at the right time. Their "creativity" is their ability to synthesize and build upon the ideas circulating within their community. (B) However, sociological research into creativity challenges this notion. It reveals that major breakthroughs and innovations are rarely the product of a single person's mind. Instead, they often emerge from collaborative networks, scenes, and communities where ideas are shared, debated, and refined. (C) For instance, the Renaissance did not arise from a few isolated artists but from the bustling workshops and intellectual circles of Florence. Similarly, Silicon Valley's technological boom was fueled by a dense network of engineers, entrepreneurs, and venture capitalists who constantly exchanged knowledge.

Vocabulary

  • prodigies영재
  • romanticizes낭만화하다
  • collaborative협력적인
  • synthesize종합하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 402]

In the field of biology, the traditional view of evolution, heavily influenced by Darwinism, is often summarized as "survival of the fittest." This concept emphasizes competition among individuals as the primary driver of evolutionary change, suggesting that organisms are in a constant struggle against one another for limited resources. (A) For example, lichens are a symbiotic partnership between a fungus and an alga, allowing them to colonize harsh environments neither could survive alone. Similarly, the human gut microbiome consists of trillions of bacteria that are essential for digestion and immunity, demonstrating a deep co-dependence. (B) This alternative view, known as symbiogenesis, proposes that long-term cooperation and merging of different species are central to the emergence of new, more complex life forms. It reframes evolution not just as a battle, but as a process of networking and collaboration. (C) However, this competition-centric model fails to fully account for the widespread phenomenon of symbiosis, or cooperation, in nature. Many biologists now argue that collaboration has been an equally, if not more, powerful force in the history of life.

Vocabulary

  • symbiosis공생
  • collaboration협력
  • lichen지의류
  • symbiogenesis공생발생
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 403]

The conventional narrative surrounding the Industrial Revolution often focuses on technological inventions and heroic entrepreneurs in Great Britain. It is commonly taught that innovations like the steam engine and the spinning jenny single-handedly sparked a new era of mass production and economic growth, positioning Britain as the workshop of the world. (A) This reinterpretation does not diminish the importance of technology, but it places it within a broader context. It suggests that the revolution was not merely an event driven by a few great minds, but a complex process rooted in deep social, political, and even colonial structures. (B) For instance, Britain's access to vast colonial markets and resources, particularly cotton from slave plantations in the Americas, was crucial. Furthermore, a stable political system and legal frameworks that protected private property provided the necessary foundation for industrial investment. (C) However, a more critical historical analysis reveals that this technology-centric view is incomplete. It overlooks the crucial social and institutional preconditions that made such technological applications possible and profitable in the first place.

Vocabulary

  • preconditions전제조건
  • colonial식민지의
  • reinterpretation재해석
  • diminish줄이다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 404]

Classical economic theory is built upon the assumption of *Homo economicus*, or the "rational man." This model posits that humans are fundamentally rational beings who consistently make decisions to maximize their personal utility or self-interest. They are believed to weigh all costs and benefits logically before acting. (A) For example, people often stick with a default option even if it's not the best one (status quo bias), or they are more affected by potential losses than equivalent gains (loss aversion). These behaviors are inconsistent with the purely rational model. (B) This new field, known as behavioral economics, integrates insights from psychology to create more realistic models of human decision-making. It doesn't discard rationality entirely but acknowledges that it is "bounded" by cognitive limitations and emotional biases. (C) However, decades of psychological research and real-world observation have shown that this assumption is deeply flawed. Human behavior is frequently driven by emotions, cognitive shortcuts, and social influences that lead to seemingly irrational choices.

Vocabulary

  • utility효용
  • flawed결함이 있는
  • statusquo bias 현상 유지 편향
  • bounded제한된
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 405]

The concept of "authenticity" in tourism is highly sought after by modern travelers. Many tourists seek to escape the curated, commercialized experiences of mass tourism and find what they believe to be the "real" or "untouched" culture of a destination. (A) This phenomenon, sometimes called "staged authenticity," means that what tourists perceive as genuine is often a performance carefully constructed by the local community or tourism industry to meet tourist expectations. The "real" culture remains backstage, away from the visitor's gaze. (B) As a result, a paradoxical situation arises. The more tourists search for authenticity, the more it is commercialized and staged for them, making the genuine article even more elusive. The very act of observing changes the nature of what is being observed. (C) However, sociologists of tourism argue that this quest for authenticity is often based on a naive illusion. They contend that in a globalized world, a truly "untouched" culture, completely isolated from outside influence, hardly exists.

Vocabulary

  • authenticity진정성
  • curated기획된
  • naive순진한
  • elusive찾기 힘든
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 406]

The concept of the "food pyramid," first introduced by the USDA in 1992, became a ubiquitous symbol of healthy eating for decades. It advised a diet high in carbohydrates like bread and pasta at its base, with fats and oils to be used sparingly at the very top. (A) For instance, it failed to distinguish between healthy complex carbohydrates (like whole grains) and unhealthy refined ones (like white bread). Similarly, it grouped all fats together, ignoring the benefits of unsaturated fats found in olive oil and nuts. (B) This led to the development of alternative models, such as Harvard's "Healthy Eating Plate," which emphasizes vegetable intake, promotes whole grains over refined ones, and encourages healthy proteins, offering a more nuanced guide to nutrition. (C) However, over time, nutrition science has revealed significant flaws in this simplistic model. Critics argue that its one-size-fits-all approach was not only overly broad but also potentially misleading, contributing to public confusion about healthy diets.

Vocabulary

  • ubiquitous어디에나 있는
  • sparingly아껴서
  • flaws결함
  • nuanced미묘한 차이가 있는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 407]

In the study of international relations, the theory of realism has long been dominant. It posits that states are the main actors on the world stage and their primary goal is survival in an anarchic system. This often leads to a focus on military power and a belief that conflict is an inevitable feature of global politics. (A) This alternative theory, known as liberalism or liberal institutionalism, argues that cooperation is possible through international institutions, economic interdependence, and the spread of democracy. These factors can mitigate the anarchic nature of the international system. (B) For example, the existence of the United Nations provides a forum for diplomacy over conflict, and the deep economic ties within the European Union make war between its members almost unthinkable. These examples challenge the realist assumption of perpetual conflict. (C) However, this bleak outlook is challenged by the observable reality of extensive international cooperation. The realist focus on conflict and power politics fails to adequately explain the long periods of peace and the dense networks of collaboration that exist between nations.

Vocabulary

  • realism현실주의
  • anarchic무정부의
  • bleak암울한
  • mitigate완화하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 408]

The history of medicine often celebrates the "magic bullet" concept: the idea that a single, powerful drug can be developed to target and destroy a specific pathogen or diseased cell without harming the host. The discovery of antibiotics like penicillin seemed to validate this approach, heralding an era of simple cures for complex diseases. (A) Instead, a new paradigm is emerging that focuses on modulating the body's own systems, such as the immune system or the microbiome. Treatments like immunotherapy for cancer or fecal transplants for gut infections represent this shift towards a more holistic, systems-based approach. (B) This realization has led to a fundamental rethinking of medical treatment. The focus is shifting away from finding a single weapon to destroy an invader and towards understanding the complex ecology of the human body and restoring its balance. (C) However, the rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria and the complexity of chronic illnesses like cancer and diabetes have revealed the limitations of this model. It is now clear that many diseases involve complex interactions between genetics, environment, and lifestyle, and cannot be solved by a single magic bullet.

Vocabulary

  • magicbullet 특효약
  • validate입증하다
  • modulating조절하는
  • holistic전체론적인
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 409]

A prevalent cultural narrative, especially in Western societies, champions the idea of "finding your passion" as the key to a fulfilling career and life. This advice suggests that we each have a pre-existing passion, and our main task is to discover it and then match it to a job. (A) This alternative mindset, often called the "craftsman mindset," suggests that passion is not something you find, but rather something you cultivate. It grows over time as you dedicate yourself to becoming exceptionally good at something valuable. (B) As a result, the advice should perhaps be "cultivate your passion" rather than "find your passion." This approach emphasizes that fulfillment comes from mastery and the sense of autonomy and impact that follows, not from an initial spark of interest. (C) However, research by psychologists like Carol Dweck suggests this "passion-finding" mindset can be counterproductive. It can lead people to give up easily when a job becomes difficult or less enjoyable, as they conclude it must not have been their true passion after all.

Vocabulary

  • prevalent널리 퍼진
  • fulfilling만족스러운
  • counterproductive역효과를 낳는
  • cultivate기르다, 함양하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 410]

The traditional model of urban planning after World War II heavily favored the automobile. Cities were designed with sprawling suburbs, wide highways, and ample parking, a system built on the assumption that private car ownership was the ultimate form of mobility and freedom. (A) This new approach, often termed "New Urbanism," promotes mixed-use development, walkable neighborhoods, and robust public transportation. The goal is to create vibrant, sustainable communities where cars are an option, not a necessity. (B) For instance, this car-centric design has led to chronic traffic congestion, significant air pollution, and social isolation in sprawling, disconnected suburbs. It also marginalizes those who cannot or choose not to drive, such as the elderly, the young, and the poor. (C) However, decades of experience with this model have exposed its severe environmental and social costs. Planners and citizens are increasingly recognizing that a city designed solely for cars is often a city that fails its human inhabitants.

Vocabulary

  • sprawling제멋대로 뻗어 나가는
  • ample충분한
  • marginalizes소외시키다
  • robust탄탄한
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 411]

The global financial system appeared remarkably stable in the mid-2000s, with many economies experiencing steady growth. However, beneath this surface of calm, a significant vulnerability was developing within the U.S. housing market, setting the stage for a worldwide crisis. (A) This chain reaction culminated in a massive credit crunch. Banks, uncertain of the value of their assets and the solvency of their counterparts, stopped lending to each other. This freeze in the credit markets crippled businesses and led to the collapse of major financial institutions, spreading panic across the globe. (B) The trigger for the crisis was the bursting of the U.S. housing bubble around 2006-2007. As housing prices fell, many homeowners with subprime mortgages found themselves owing more than their homes were worth. This led to a wave of defaults, rendering the mortgage-backed securities tied to them nearly worthless. (C) The root of this vulnerability was the proliferation of subprime mortgages—loans given to borrowers with poor credit histories. These risky loans were bundled together into complex financial instruments called mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and sold to investors worldwide, who were attracted by their high yields.

Vocabulary

  • vulnerability취약성
  • proliferation확산
  • subprime서브프라임의
  • solvency지불 능력
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 412]

The removal of a top predator from an ecosystem can initiate a series of dramatic and unforeseen changes. This phenomenon, known as a trophic cascade, demonstrates the profound interconnectedness of species within a food web, where the absence of one key player can alter the entire landscape. (A) Consequently, the physical environment begins to change. The loss of vegetation along riverbanks leads to increased soil erosion, altering the course of rivers. The decline in plant life also reduces the habitat and food sources for other species, such as birds and insects, causing a significant drop in local biodiversity. (B) With their primary predator gone, the population of herbivores, such as deer or elk, often experiences explosive growth. No longer held in check, their numbers swell far beyond the ecosystem's carrying capacity, putting immense pressure on the local vegetation. (C) This unchecked herbivore population then proceeds to overgraze the area. They consume saplings, shrubs, and grasses at an unsustainable rate, preventing forests from regenerating and stripping the landscape bare.

Vocabulary

  • trophiccascade 영양 폭포
  • herbivores초식동물
  • overgraze과도하게 방목하다
  • erosion침식
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 413]

When a pressing social issue arises that existing laws do not adequately address, the legislative process to create a new law is often initiated. This complex journey from an idea to an enforceable statute involves several distinct stages of deliberation and approval. (A) Following its introduction, the bill is assigned to a specific committee that holds expertise in the relevant area. Here, it undergoes intense scrutiny, with public hearings, expert testimony, and proposed amendments. If the committee votes to approve it, the bill is then sent to the floor of the full legislative body for open debate and a final vote. (B) The first step is the drafting of a bill. A legislator, or a group of them, works with legal experts to write the proposed law's text. Once drafted, the bill is formally introduced on the floor of either the House or the Senate, where it is assigned a number and officially enters the legislative record. (C) If the bill successfully passes the vote in both legislative chambers, a final version is prepared and sent to the executive branch—the President or Governor. The executive then has the authority to sign the bill into law or to veto it, sending it back to the legislature.

Vocabulary

  • legislative입법의
  • statute법규
  • scrutiny정밀 조사
  • veto거부권
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 414]

The development of type 2 diabetes is a gradual process that often begins long before a formal diagnosis. It starts with a condition known as insulin resistance, where the body's cells do not respond effectively to the hormone insulin. (A) Over time, this constant high demand for insulin places a severe strain on the pancreas. The insulin-producing beta cells in the pancreas struggle to keep up and eventually begin to wear out, leading to a decline in the body's ability to produce sufficient amounts of the hormone. (B) In this initial stage, the pancreas compensates for the cells' poor response by producing more and more insulin. This extra output helps to force glucose into the cells, successfully keeping blood sugar levels within a normal range, often for many years. (C) With both insulin resistance persisting and insulin production falling, the system finally fails. Blood sugar can no longer be controlled, and it rises to unhealthy levels. This state of chronic high blood sugar, or hyperglycemia, marks the official onset of type 2 diabetes and its associated health risks.

Vocabulary

  • insulinresistance 인슐린 저항성
  • compensates보상하다
  • strain부담
  • hyperglycemia고혈당증
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 415]

A company often embarks on the journey of creating a new product after its market research team identifies a significant consumer need or a gap in the current market. This initial insight is the catalyst for a long and resource-intensive development process. (A) Once a functional prototype is developed, it enters a crucial phase of market testing. The product is released to a limited audience, often called beta testers, whose feedback is meticulously collected. This input is vital for identifying design flaws, usability issues, and potential improvements before committing to large-scale production. (B) Based on this insight, the company allocates a budget for research and development (R&D). In this stage, engineers, designers, and scientists collaborate to transform the abstract idea into a tangible concept and eventually a working prototype. The primary goal is to ensure technical feasibility and core functionality. (C) After incorporating the feedback and finalizing the design, the company moves to the final stage: mass production and market launch. Manufacturing facilities are prepared, supply chains are established, and a comprehensive marketing strategy is executed to build awareness and drive initial sales as the product is officially introduced to the public.

Vocabulary

  • catalyst촉매제
  • allocates할당하다
  • prototype시제품
  • meticulously꼼꼼하게
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 416]

The diffusion of innovations is a theory that seeks to explain how, why, and at what rate new ideas and technology spread through cultures. The process is not instantaneous; rather, it involves a series of adopter categories, each with distinct characteristics, who adopt the innovation over time. (A) Following them are the early majority and late majority, who are more pragmatic and skeptical, respectively. They adopt the innovation only after it has been proven and has become a mainstream standard. This large group is critical for the innovation to achieve widespread success. (B) The process begins with a small group of venturesome individuals known as innovators. They are risk-takers who are eager to try new ideas. Soon after, the early adopters, who are respected opinion leaders in their communities, begin to use the innovation, increasing its visibility and appeal. (C) Finally, at the end of the spectrum, are the laggards. This group is the most traditional and resistant to change. They will only adopt an innovation when it has become a part of everyday life or, in some cases, they may never adopt it at all.

Vocabulary

  • diffusion확산
  • venturesome모험적인
  • pragmatic실용적인
  • laggards지체자
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 417]

The end of World War II marked a turning point in global history, significantly weakening the old European colonial empires. This new geopolitical reality created a fertile ground for colonized peoples around the world to demand their freedom. (A) In response to this growing pressure, both internal and international, the colonial powers began the process of decolonization. They started granting independence to their colonies, a transition that was peaceful in some cases but marked by violent conflict in others, leading to the birth of dozens of new sovereign nations. (B) This period saw the rapid rise of nationalist movements within the colonies. Local leaders, many of whom were educated in the West, began to powerfully advocate for self-determination. They organized political parties, led protests, and mobilized the populace to challenge colonial rule. (C) However, independence did not automatically lead to prosperity or stability. These newly formed nations often faced immense post-colonial challenges, including political instability, ethnic strife fueled by arbitrary colonial borders, and economic dependence on their former rulers, hindering their development for decades to come.

Vocabulary

  • geopolitical지정학적인
  • nationalist민족주의자
  • decolonization탈식민지화
  • strife갈등
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 418]

When confronted with a profound loss, such as the death of a close family member, individuals often navigate a complex and painful emotional journey. This process, while unique to each person, can often be understood through a series of common stages. (A) This is often followed by a period of bargaining, where the individual may attempt to make a deal with a higher power to reverse the loss. When this fails, the full weight of the situation sets in, leading to a stage of deep sadness and depression. (B) The final stage is acceptance. This does not necessarily mean happiness, but rather an understanding of the reality of the loss and a recognition that life must continue. It is about learning to live with the new reality, integrating the loss into one's life, and beginning to move forward. (C) Initially, there is often a stage of denial, a defense mechanism where the person cannot accept the reality of what has happened. As the initial shock wears off, this denial can give way to anger, with the individual lashing out and questioning, "Why me?"

Vocabulary

  • confrontedwith ~에 직면한
  • denial부정
  • bargaining타협
  • acceptance수용
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 419]

The process of gentrification describes the transformation of a low-income urban neighborhood as wealthier individuals and new businesses move in. It typically begins subtly in areas that have experienced disinvestment and are perceived as undesirable by the mainstream. (A) As the neighborhood's reputation grows, real estate developers and more affluent homebuyers take notice. This influx of capital triggers a rapid increase in property values and rents. Consequently, the original, lower-income residents and long-standing small businesses are often priced out and forced to relocate. (B) The first wave often consists of "pioneers" like artists, students, and young professionals attracted by the cheap rent and unique character of the area. Their presence, along with the opening of a few trendy cafes or art galleries, starts to slowly change the neighborhood's image and attract outside interest. (C) In the final phase, the neighborhood's transformation is complete. The area is now characterized by luxury condos, high-end retail stores, and a demographic makeup that is significantly wealthier than before. The original cultural and social fabric of the community has been almost entirely replaced.

Vocabulary

  • gentrification젠트리피케이션
  • disinvestment투자 회수
  • affluent부유한
  • relocate이주하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 420]

Acid rain is a broad term referring to precipitation with high levels of nitric and sulfuric acids. This environmental problem originates from human activities and has a destructive impact on nature and the built environment alike. (A) In the atmosphere, these primary pollutants react with water, oxygen, and other chemicals to form secondary pollutants, namely sulfuric acid and nitric acid. Strong prevailing winds can transport these acidic compounds over hundreds of kilometers. (B) The process is initiated when fossil fuels, such as coal and oil, are burned in power plants and industrial facilities. This combustion releases enormous quantities of sulfur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides (NOx) directly into the air. (C) Eventually, these acids fall back to Earth, either in wet form as rain, snow, or fog, or in dry form as acidic gases and particles. This acid deposition can have severe consequences, such as damaging forests, acidifying lakes to the point that they cannot support life, and corroding man-made structures.

Vocabulary

  • precipitation강수
  • combustion연소
  • prevailingwinds 편서풍
  • corroding부식시키는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 421]

The philosophy of Existentialism, which gained prominence in the mid-20th century, fundamentally posits that existence precedes essence. This means that individuals are born without a predefined purpose or nature and must create their own meaning in an otherwise meaningless world. (A) This initial phase, often associated with figures like Søren Kierkegaard and Friedrich Nietzsche, explored themes of individuality, passion, and the "death of God," challenging traditional metaphysical and religious certainties. They laid the groundwork for what was to come. (B) Building upon this foundation, Jean-Paul Sartre, a key figure in the movement, famously articulated the principle that "man is nothing else but what he makes of himself." He argued that humans are "condemned to be free," bearing the full responsibility for their choices and actions without excuse. (C) The historical roots of this thought can be traced back to the 19th century, long before the term "Existentialism" was coined. It began not as a formal school of thought but as a reaction against the abstract and systematic philosophies of thinkers like Hegel.

Vocabulary

  • prominence명성, 중요성
  • precedes~에 앞서다
  • metaphysical형이상학의
  • articulated명확히 표현했다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 422]

The transition from Romanesque to Gothic architecture in medieval Europe marked a revolutionary shift in building design and engineering. This evolution, occurring roughly between the 12th and 16th centuries, was driven by a desire for taller, brighter, and more awe-inspiring churches. (A) The visual and spiritual effect of these innovations was profound. The dark, heavy, and earthbound feel of Romanesque churches was replaced by the light-filled, soaring, and skeletal grace of Gothic cathedrals, designed to draw the worshiper's gaze and spirit upward toward the heavens. (B) This ambition was realized through a cluster of key structural innovations. The most crucial were the pointed arch, the ribbed vault, and the flying buttress. Unlike the rounded arches of Romanesque buildings, the pointed arch could support more weight and direct pressure downwards more efficiently. (C) Consequently, these elements worked in concert to create a new kind of skeletal structure. The flying buttresses supported the high walls from the outside, allowing the walls themselves to be thinner and pierced by vast expanses of stained-glass windows, which flooded the interior with colored light.

Vocabulary

  • awe-inspiring경외심을 불러일으키는
  • ribbedvault 늑골 궁륭
  • flyingbuttress 공중 부벽
  • skeletal골격의
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 423]

The understanding of the universe underwent a monumental paradigm shift with the transition from the geocentric to the heliocentric model. For over 1,500 years, the Ptolemaic system, which placed Earth at the center of the cosmos, was the accepted astronomical authority. (A) However, it was Galileo Galilei who provided the crucial observational evidence that supported the Copernican theory. Using his newly invented telescope, he observed the phases of Venus and the moons of Jupiter, which were phenomena that could not be reconciled with the traditional Earth-centered model. (B) This long-standing view was first seriously challenged in the 16th century by Nicolaus Copernicus. In his work "De revolutionibus orbium coelestium," he proposed a heliocentric model, positing that the Sun, not the Earth, was the center of the universe and that the planets revolved around it. (C) Galileo's findings, while revolutionary, were met with fierce opposition from the academic and religious establishment, leading to his famous trial. Nevertheless, his empirical proof marked a turning point, paving the way for the eventual triumph of heliocentrism and the birth of modern astronomy.

Vocabulary

  • geocentric지구 중심의
  • heliocentric태양 중심의
  • reconciled조화된
  • empirical경험적인
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 424]

The history of the English language is marked by several transformative periods, but few are as significant as the Great Vowel Shift. This was a series of profound changes in the pronunciation of long vowels that took place in Southern England between the 15th and 18th centuries. (A) For instance, the Middle English word for "house," pronounced /huːs/ (like "hoos"), shifted so that the vowel sound moved higher and to the front of the mouth, eventually becoming the modern /haʊs/. Similarly, the word "name," once pronounced /naːmə/ (with an "ah" sound), evolved to its current pronunciation /neɪm/. (B) The primary effect of this phonological change was that the seven long vowels of Middle English were systematically raised or diphthongized. In simple terms, vowels that were produced low in the mouth began to be articulated higher up, and the highest vowels became diphthongs (a combination of two vowel sounds). (C) These changes are a major reason why the spelling of many English words does not seem to correspond to their modern pronunciation. The English spelling system was becoming standardized just as the Great Vowel Shift was beginning, effectively freezing a pronunciation that would soon become obsolete.

Vocabulary

  • vowel모음
  • phonological음운론적인
  • diphthongized이중모음화된
  • correspondto ~에 부합하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 425]

The evolution of computer technology is often categorized into generations, each defined by a major technological development that fundamentally changed the way computers operated. The first generation set the stage, but subsequent generations brought exponential increases in power and accessibility. (A) Following this, the third generation (1964-1971) emerged with the development of the integrated circuit (IC). This innovation involved placing thousands of transistors onto a single silicon chip, making computers smaller, faster, and more reliable than ever before. (B) The second generation of computers (1956-1963) was characterized by the replacement of bulky vacuum tubes with the transistor. Invented at Bell Labs, the transistor was far superior: it was smaller, consumed less power, generated less heat, and was significantly more dependable. (C) Finally, the fourth generation, beginning in 1971, was marked by the invention of the microprocessor. This single chip contained all the essential components of a computer—the central processing unit (CPU), memory, and input/output controls—leading directly to the personal computer (PC) revolution.

Vocabulary

  • subsequent그 후의
  • bulky부피가 큰
  • integratedcircuit 집적 회로
  • microprocessor마이크로프로세서
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 426]

The art movement known as Impressionism, which emerged in France in the late 19th century, represented a radical departure from the established traditions of European painting. The artists involved sought to capture the fleeting, sensory effect of a scene—the "impression" objects made on the eye in a moment. (A) This new approach was met with harsh criticism from the conservative art establishment, particularly the Académie des Beaux-Arts, which dominated the art world. The term "Impressionism" itself was originally coined by a critic as a term of derision after viewing Claude Monet's painting, *Impression, soleil levant*. (B) To achieve this, they moved out of the studio and into the open air, a practice known as *en plein air*. They used visible brushstrokes, an emphasis on the accurate depiction of light in its changing qualities, and a bright, vibrant palette of colors that was shocking to contemporary audiences. (C) Despite the initial hostility, the Impressionists organized their own independent exhibitions, and their revolutionary vision eventually gained acceptance. The movement paved the way for numerous modern art styles, fundamentally altering the course of Western art by prioritizing subjective perception over objective representation.

Vocabulary

  • fleeting덧없는
  • derision조롱
  • hostility적대감
  • subjectiveperception 주관적 인식
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 427]

The development of the symphony from its humble origins to the monumental form we know today is a cornerstone of Western classical music history. Initially, the term did not refer to the grand, multi-movement work it would become. (A) It was during the Classical period, with composers like Joseph Haydn and Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, that the symphony was codified into its standard four-movement structure. Haydn, often called the "Father of the Symphony," composed over a hundred, establishing the formal conventions that would define the genre for decades. (B) This transformation was propelled further by Ludwig van Beethoven. He expanded the symphony's emotional range, length, and instrumentation, famously incorporating a chorus and vocal soloists into his Ninth Symphony, thereby pushing the boundaries of what the form could express. (C) In the Baroque era, the "sinfonia" was often a short instrumental piece used as an overture to an opera or oratorio. It typically consisted of three short sections in a fast-slow-fast pattern, serving a purely introductory function.

Vocabulary

  • humble미천한
  • codified성문화된
  • propelled나아가게 했다
  • overture서곡
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 428]

The discovery of germ theory in the 19th century represents one of the most significant breakthroughs in the history of medicine. It fundamentally altered humanity's understanding of disease, shifting the focus from supernatural causes or imbalances in the body to microscopic organisms. (A) Building on Pasteur's work, Robert Koch developed a rigorous set of criteria, known as Koch's postulates, to definitively link a specific microbe to a specific disease. His work identifying the bacteria responsible for anthrax, tuberculosis, and cholera provided undeniable proof of the theory. (B) Before this theory was established, the dominant explanation for disease transmission was the miasma theory, which held that illnesses were caused by "bad air" emanating from decaying organic matter. This belief guided public health interventions for centuries, often with limited success. (C) The paradigm shift began with the pioneering experiments of Louis Pasteur. Through his work on fermentation and pasteurization, he demonstrated that microorganisms, not spontaneous generation, were responsible for chemical changes and could cause spoilage. This led him to propose that these "germs" could also cause diseases in humans.

Vocabulary

  • germtheory 세균설
  • postulates공리, 원칙
  • miasma독기, 나쁜 공기
  • fermentation발효
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 429]

The economic doctrine of Mercantilism dominated European thought and policy from the 16th to the 18th century. It was based on the core belief that a nation's wealth and power were best served by increasing exports and collecting precious metals, like gold and silver. (A) He argued that wealth was not finite but could be created through labor and production. In his seminal work, "The Wealth of Nations," he introduced the concept of the "invisible hand," suggesting that free markets, driven by self-interest, would ultimately benefit society as a whole. (B) This worldview led to policies of protectionism, such as high tariffs on imported goods and the establishment of colonies to serve as sources of raw materials and markets for finished products. The goal was to maintain a positive balance of trade at all costs. (C) However, this system faced a powerful intellectual challenge in the late 18th century from Adam Smith, the father of modern economics. Smith fundamentally disagreed with the mercantilist premise that global wealth was a zero-sum game.

Vocabulary

  • Mercantilism중상주의
  • protectionism보호무역주의
  • premise전제
  • zero-sumgame 제로섬 게임
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 430]

The concept of the social contract has been a foundational element of Western political philosophy, offering a rationale for the legitimacy of state authority over the individual. It generally posits that individuals have consented, either explicitly or tacitly, to surrender some of their freedoms to a ruling authority in exchange for the protection of their remaining rights. (A) In stark contrast, John Locke presented a different version of this contract. For Locke, the state of nature was governed by natural law, and individuals possessed inherent rights to life, liberty, and property. People form governments to protect these pre-existing rights, and if a government violates the contract, the people have the right to revolution. (B) Thomas Hobbes, writing in the context of the English Civil War, provided one of the earliest and most influential formulations. He argued that in a "state of nature," life would be "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short." To escape this chaos, people rationally agree to submit to an absolute sovereign who can guarantee peace and order. (C) The differences between these two interpretations have had a profound and lasting impact on political thought. Hobbes's version provides a strong justification for authoritarian rule, while Locke's ideas became a cornerstone of modern liberal democracy, heavily influencing the American Declaration of Independence.

Vocabulary

  • rationale근거, 이유
  • tacitly암묵적으로
  • sovereign주권자
  • authoritarian권위주의적인
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 431]

Jean Piaget, a Swiss psychologist, proposed a groundbreaking theory on the cognitive development of children. He argued that children are not simply miniature adults but that their thinking processes are fundamentally different and evolve through a series of distinct stages. (A) The first of these is the sensorimotor stage, from birth to about two years of age. During this period, infants primarily learn about the world through their senses and motor activities. A key achievement here is object permanence—the understanding that objects continue to exist even when they cannot be seen. (B) Following this, the preoperational stage (ages 2-7) emerges. Children in this stage begin to think symbolically and use words and pictures to represent objects. However, their thinking is still egocentric, meaning they struggle to see things from the perspective of others. (C) Finally, the concrete operational (ages 7-11) and formal operational (age 12 and up) stages follow. The former is marked by the development of logical thought about concrete events, while the latter involves the ability to think abstractly and reason about hypothetical problems. These stages illustrate a progressive sophistication in a child's cognitive abilities.

Vocabulary

  • groundbreaking획기적인
  • sensorimotor감각운동의
  • egocentric자기중심적인
  • hypothetical가설의
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 432]

Photosynthesis is the vital process used by plants, algae, and certain bacteria to convert light energy into chemical energy. This process is fundamental to life on Earth, as it produces oxygen and provides the primary energy source for most ecosystems. The overall process can be divided into two main stages. (A) The first stage is the light-dependent reactions, which occur in the thylakoid membranes of chloroplasts. In this stage, chlorophyll absorbs energy from sunlight. This energy is then used to split water molecules into oxygen, protons, and electrons. (B) These energy-carrying molecules, ATP and NADPH, are then utilized in the second stage. This stage does not directly require light and is known as the light-independent reactions or the Calvin cycle. (C) The energy captured from sunlight is converted into chemical energy in the form of two molecules: adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH). They act as temporary energy stores.

Vocabulary

  • chloroplasts엽록체
  • thylakoid틸라코이드
  • adenosinetriphosphate 아데노신 삼인산
  • utilized활용된
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 433]

The life cycle of a star is a fascinating cosmic journey that spans billions of years. A star's mass is the primary factor determining its evolution, from its birth in a stellar nursery to its eventual demise. Let's consider the life path of a sun-like star. (A) As the core temperature and pressure rise sufficiently, nuclear fusion begins. In this process, hydrogen atoms fuse to form helium, releasing an immense amount of energy. This energy pushes outward, balancing the inward pull of gravity and marking the birth of a main-sequence star. (B) It all begins within a nebula, a vast cloud of gas and dust. Gravity causes denser regions of this cloud to collapse under their own weight. As the material contracts, it forms a hot, dense core known as a protostar. (C) Eventually, the star exhausts its hydrogen fuel. The core contracts and heats up, causing the outer layers to expand and cool, transforming it into a red giant. After this phase, the outer layers drift away, leaving behind a dense, hot core called a white dwarf, which slowly cools over eons.

Vocabulary

  • demise종말
  • nebula성운
  • protostar원시별
  • eons영겁
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 434]

The rock cycle is a fundamental concept in geology that describes the dynamic transitions through geologic time among the three main rock types: igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic. This cycle illustrates how rocks are continuously being changed from one type to another. (A) Over time, these fragments are carried away by wind or water and deposited in layers. As more layers accumulate, the pressure compacts the lower layers, and dissolved minerals cement the fragments together, forming sedimentary rock. (B) Furthermore, if this rock is subjected to intense heat and pressure deep within the Earth's crust, it can change once again. This process, called metamorphism, transforms it into metamorphic rock without melting it. (C) The cycle can begin with magma, which is molten rock from beneath the Earth's surface. When this magma cools and solidifies, it forms igneous rock. This rock is then exposed to the elements, leading to weathering and erosion, which break it down into smaller fragments called sediment.

Vocabulary

  • igneous화성의
  • sedimentary퇴적의
  • metamorphic변성의
  • weathering풍화
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 435]

The scientific method is a systematic procedure that has characterized natural science since the 17th century. It consists of systematic observation, measurement, and experiment, and the formulation, testing, and modification of hypotheses. It provides a logical framework for inquiry. (A) Based on the results, the scientist analyzes the data to draw a conclusion. This conclusion either supports or refutes the hypothesis. If the hypothesis is refuted, it must be modified or rejected, often leading to a new cycle of observation and experimentation. (B) The process typically begins when a scientist makes an observation about the natural world that raises a question. To answer this question, the scientist formulates a testable explanation, known as a hypothesis. (C) Next, the scientist designs and conducts a controlled experiment to test it. The experiment must be carefully designed to isolate and test a single variable, and the data gathered must be objective and measurable.

Vocabulary

  • systematic체계적인
  • formulation공식화
  • hypothesis가설
  • refutes반박하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 436]

The Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) is a structured process that enables the production of high-quality, low-cost software in the shortest possible production time. The goal of the SDLC is to produce superior software that meets and exceeds all customer expectations and demands. (A) Based on these requirements, the design phase begins. Architects and developers create a detailed blueprint for the system, outlining the architecture, user interface, and data models. This design acts as a guide for the next stage. (B) Following the design, the implementation or coding phase occurs, where developers write the actual code. After coding, the software undergoes rigorous testing to identify and fix defects, ensuring it functions as intended before it is deployed to users. (C) The first phase in the SDLC is requirements gathering and analysis. During this stage, project managers and analysts collect detailed information from all stakeholders to understand the business needs and what the system must accomplish.

Vocabulary

  • structured구조화된
  • blueprint청사진
  • rigorous엄격한
  • stakeholders이해관계자
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 437]

Eutrophication is an environmental issue caused by the enrichment of an ecosystem with chemical nutrients, typically compounds containing nitrogen or phosphorus. While it can occur naturally, it is often accelerated by human activities, leading to severe negative consequences for aquatic environments. (A) The decomposition of this massive amount of dead algae by bacteria consumes large quantities of dissolved oxygen in the water. This oxygen depletion creates a "dead zone," a hypoxic condition where fish and other aquatic organisms cannot survive. (B) The process often begins when excess nutrients from sources like agricultural fertilizers and urban sewage are washed into a body of water, such as a lake or river. (C) These nutrients act as a fertilizer for algae and aquatic plants, triggering a rapid and excessive growth known as an algal bloom. This bloom covers the water's surface, blocking sunlight from reaching other plants below and disrupting the ecosystem's balance.

Vocabulary

  • eutrophication부영양화
  • enrichment비옥화
  • decomposition분해
  • hypoxic저산소의
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 438]

Blood clotting, or coagulation, is a crucial physiological process that prevents excessive bleeding when a blood vessel is injured. It is a complex cascade of events involving various cellular components and proteins in the blood, culminating in the formation of a solid clot. (A) Immediately after an injury to a blood vessel, platelets in the blood adhere to the site of injury. They change shape and release chemical signals that attract more platelets, forming a temporary plug. (B) Once bleeding has stopped and the vessel has had time to heal, this clot is no longer needed. The body then initiates another process to dissolve it, ensuring that blood flow is restored to normal. (C) This platelet plug is then reinforced by a mesh of protein strands called fibrin. The formation of this mesh is the result of a complex series of chemical reactions known as the coagulation cascade, which transforms a soluble protein called fibrinogen into insoluble fibrin.

Vocabulary

  • coagulation응고
  • platelets혈소판
  • adhere부착하다
  • fibrin피브린(섬유소)
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 439]

The practice of mummification in ancient Egypt was a sophisticated and ritualistic process developed to preserve the body for the afterlife. The Egyptians believed that the soul needed its physical body to navigate the underworld, making preservation paramount. The entire procedure was carried out by specialized priests over a period of 70 days. (A) The next crucial step was dehydration. The body was covered and packed inside and out with natron, a type of natural salt. This substance absorbed all the moisture from the body, which was essential for preventing decay. This stage lasted for about 40 days. (B) Finally, after the body was completely dry, the priests would wrap it from head to toe in hundreds of yards of linen bandages. Amulets were often placed between the layers for protection, and a final shroud was placed over the wrapped body before it was placed in a coffin. (C) The process began with the purification of the body. Then, the embalmers made an incision on the left side of the abdomen to remove most of the internal organs, such as the stomach, intestines, liver, and lungs. The brain was typically removed through the nostrils using a long hook.

Vocabulary

  • ritualistic의식적인
  • paramount가장 중요한
  • dehydration탈수
  • amulets부적
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 440]

A nuclear reactor is the heart of a nuclear power plant, designed to initiate and control a sustained nuclear chain reaction. The primary purpose of a reactor is to generate a vast amount of heat, which is then used to produce electricity through a series of transformations. (A) This heat is used to boil water, creating high-pressure steam. The process is similar to how a conventional power plant burns fossil fuels to create steam, but the heat source is nuclear fission instead of combustion. (B) The process starts in the reactor core, where nuclear fission occurs. In this reaction, the nucleus of a heavy atom, such as uranium-235, is split when it absorbs a neutron. This splitting releases a tremendous amount of energy in the form of heat, along with more neutrons. (C) This steam is then channeled to a turbine. The force of the expanding steam pushes against the blades of the turbine, causing it to spin rapidly. This mechanical energy is then used to power a generator, which ultimately produces electricity.

Vocabulary

  • sustained지속적인
  • fission핵분열
  • combustion연소
  • turbine터빈
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 441]

It is a common social belief that creativity is the domain of lone geniuses, individuals who, through some innate and mysterious talent, produce groundbreaking works of art or science. This "lone genius" myth romanticizes the creative process, portraying it as a flash of inspiration that strikes an isolated individual. (A) This perspective suggests that what we often label as individual genius is, in fact, the outcome of a person being in the right network at the right time. Their "creativity" is their ability to synthesize and build upon the ideas circulating within their community. (B) However, sociological research into creativity challenges this notion. It reveals that major breakthroughs and innovations are rarely the product of a single person's mind. Instead, they often emerge from collaborative networks, scenes, and communities where ideas are shared, debated, and refined. (C) For instance, the Renaissance did not arise from a few isolated artists but from the bustling workshops and intellectual circles of Florence. Similarly, Silicon Valley's technological boom was fueled by a dense network of engineers, entrepreneurs, and venture capitalists who constantly exchanged knowledge.

Vocabulary

  • prodigies영재
  • romanticizes낭만화하다
  • collaborative협력적인
  • synthesize종합하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 442]

In the field of biology, the traditional view of evolution, heavily influenced by Darwinism, is often summarized as "survival of the fittest." This concept emphasizes competition among individuals as the primary driver of evolutionary change, suggesting that organisms are in a constant struggle against one another for limited resources. (A) For example, lichens are a symbiotic partnership between a fungus and an alga, allowing them to colonize harsh environments neither could survive alone. Similarly, the human gut microbiome consists of trillions of bacteria that are essential for digestion and immunity, demonstrating a deep co-dependence. (B) This alternative view, known as symbiogenesis, proposes that long-term cooperation and merging of different species are central to the emergence of new, more complex life forms. It reframes evolution not just as a battle, but as a process of networking and collaboration. (C) However, this competition-centric model fails to fully account for the widespread phenomenon of symbiosis, or cooperation, in nature. Many biologists now argue that collaboration has been an equally, if not more, powerful force in the history of life.

Vocabulary

  • symbiosis공생
  • collaboration협력
  • lichen지의류
  • symbiogenesis공생발생
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 443]

The conventional narrative surrounding the Industrial Revolution often focuses on technological inventions and heroic entrepreneurs in Great Britain. It is commonly taught that innovations like the steam engine and the spinning jenny single-handedly sparked a new era of mass production and economic growth, positioning Britain as the workshop of the world. (A) This reinterpretation does not diminish the importance of technology, but it places it within a broader context. It suggests that the revolution was not merely an event driven by a few great minds, but a complex process rooted in deep social, political, and even colonial structures. (B) For instance, Britain's access to vast colonial markets and resources, particularly cotton from slave plantations in the Americas, was crucial. Furthermore, a stable political system and legal frameworks that protected private property provided the necessary foundation for industrial investment. (C) However, a more critical historical analysis reveals that this technology-centric view is incomplete. It overlooks the crucial social and institutional preconditions that made such technological applications possible and profitable in the first place.

Vocabulary

  • preconditions전제조건
  • colonial식민지의
  • reinterpretation재해석
  • diminish줄이다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 444]

Classical economic theory is built upon the assumption of *Homo economicus*, or the "rational man." This model posits that humans are fundamentally rational beings who consistently make decisions to maximize their personal utility or self-interest. They are believed to weigh all costs and benefits logically before acting. (A) For example, people often stick with a default option even if it's not the best one (status quo bias), or they are more affected by potential losses than equivalent gains (loss aversion). These behaviors are inconsistent with the purely rational model. (B) This new field, known as behavioral economics, integrates insights from psychology to create more realistic models of human decision-making. It doesn't discard rationality entirely but acknowledges that it is "bounded" by cognitive limitations and emotional biases. (C) However, decades of psychological research and real-world observation have shown that this assumption is deeply flawed. Human behavior is frequently driven by emotions, cognitive shortcuts, and social influences that lead to seemingly irrational choices.

Vocabulary

  • utility효용
  • flawed결함이 있는
  • statusquo bias 현상 유지 편향
  • bounded제한된
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 445]

The concept of "authenticity" in tourism is highly sought after by modern travelers. Many tourists seek to escape the curated, commercialized experiences of mass tourism and find what they believe to be the "real" or "untouched" culture of a destination. (A) This phenomenon, sometimes called "staged authenticity," means that what tourists perceive as genuine is often a performance carefully constructed by the local community or tourism industry to meet tourist expectations. The "real" culture remains backstage, away from the visitor's gaze. (B) As a result, a paradoxical situation arises. The more tourists search for authenticity, the more it is commercialized and staged for them, making the genuine article even more elusive. The very act of observing changes the nature of what is being observed. (C) However, sociologists of tourism argue that this quest for authenticity is often based on a naive illusion. They contend that in a globalized world, a truly "untouched" culture, completely isolated from outside influence, hardly exists.

Vocabulary

  • authenticity진정성
  • curated기획된
  • naive순진한
  • elusive찾기 힘든
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 446]

The concept of the "food pyramid," first introduced by the USDA in 1992, became a ubiquitous symbol of healthy eating for decades. It advised a diet high in carbohydrates like bread and pasta at its base, with fats and oils to be used sparingly at the very top. (A) For instance, it failed to distinguish between healthy complex carbohydrates (like whole grains) and unhealthy refined ones (like white bread). Similarly, it grouped all fats together, ignoring the benefits of unsaturated fats found in olive oil and nuts. (B) This led to the development of alternative models, such as Harvard's "Healthy Eating Plate," which emphasizes vegetable intake, promotes whole grains over refined ones, and encourages healthy proteins, offering a more nuanced guide to nutrition. (C) However, over time, nutrition science has revealed significant flaws in this simplistic model. Critics argue that its one-size-fits-all approach was not only overly broad but also potentially misleading, contributing to public confusion about healthy diets.

Vocabulary

  • ubiquitous어디에나 있는
  • sparingly아껴서
  • flaws결함
  • nuanced미묘한 차이가 있는

[지문 447]

In the study of international relations, the theory of realism has long been dominant. It posits that states are the main actors on the world stage and their primary goal is survival in an anarchic system. This often leads to a focus on military power and a belief that conflict is an inevitable feature of global politics. (A) This alternative theory, known as liberalism or liberal institutionalism, argues that cooperation is possible through international institutions, economic interdependence, and the spread of democracy. These factors can mitigate the anarchic nature of the international system. (B) For example, the existence of the United Nations provides a forum for diplomacy over conflict, and the deep economic ties within the European Union make war between its members almost unthinkable. These examples challenge the realist assumption of perpetual conflict. (C) However, this bleak outlook is challenged by the observable reality of extensive international cooperation. The realist focus on conflict and power politics fails to adequately explain the long periods of peace and the dense networks of collaboration that exist between nations.

Vocabulary

  • realism현실주의
  • anarchic무정부의
  • bleak암울한
  • mitigate완화하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 448]

The history of medicine often celebrates the "magic bullet" concept: the idea that a single, powerful drug can be developed to target and destroy a specific pathogen or diseased cell without harming the host. The discovery of antibiotics like penicillin seemed to validate this approach, heralding an era of simple cures for complex diseases. (A) Instead, a new paradigm is emerging that focuses on modulating the body's own systems, such as the immune system or the microbiome. Treatments like immunotherapy for cancer or fecal transplants for gut infections represent this shift towards a more holistic, systems-based approach. (B) This realization has led to a fundamental rethinking of medical treatment. The focus is shifting away from finding a single weapon to destroy an invader and towards understanding the complex ecology of the human body and restoring its balance. (C) However, the rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria and the complexity of chronic illnesses like cancer and diabetes have revealed the limitations of this model. It is now clear that many diseases involve complex interactions between genetics, environment, and lifestyle, and cannot be solved by a single magic bullet.

Vocabulary

  • magicbullet 특효약
  • validate입증하다
  • modulating조절하는
  • holistic전체론적인

[지문 449]

A prevalent cultural narrative, especially in Western societies, champions the idea of "finding your passion" as the key to a fulfilling career and life. This advice suggests that we each have a pre-existing passion, and our main task is to discover it and then match it to a job. (A) This alternative mindset, often called the "craftsman mindset," suggests that passion is not something you find, but rather something you cultivate. It grows over time as you dedicate yourself to becoming exceptionally good at something valuable. (B) As a result, the advice should perhaps be "cultivate your passion" rather than "find your passion." This approach emphasizes that fulfillment comes from mastery and the sense of autonomy and impact that follows, not from an initial spark of interest. (C) However, research by psychologists like Carol Dweck suggests this "passion-finding" mindset can be counterproductive. It can lead people to give up easily when a job becomes difficult or less enjoyable, as they conclude it must not have been their true passion after all.

Vocabulary

  • prevalent널리 퍼진
  • fulfilling만족스러운
  • counterproductive역효과를 낳는
  • cultivate기르다, 함양하다

[지문 450]

The traditional model of urban planning after World War II heavily favored the automobile. Cities were designed with sprawling suburbs, wide highways, and ample parking, a system built on the assumption that private car ownership was the ultimate form of mobility and freedom. (A) This new approach, often termed "New Urbanism," promotes mixed-use development, walkable neighborhoods, and robust public transportation. The goal is to create vibrant, sustainable communities where cars are an option, not a necessity. (B) For instance, this car-centric design has led to chronic traffic congestion, significant air pollution, and social isolation in sprawling, disconnected suburbs. It also marginalizes those who cannot or choose not to drive, such as the elderly, the young, and the poor. (C) However, decades of experience with this model have exposed its severe environmental and social costs. Planners and citizens are increasingly recognizing that a city designed solely for cars is often a city that fails its human inhabitants.

  • sprawling제멋대로 뻗어 나가는
  • ample충분한

[지문 451]

Making authentic Italian tiramisu at home is a rewarding experience. It requires careful layering and the right ingredients to achieve its signature rich and creamy texture. (A) Finally, dust the top generously with cocoa powder. For the best flavor, the tiramisu must be refrigerated for at least four hours, or preferably overnight. This allows the flavors to meld together and the ladyfingers to soften perfectly. (B) First, prepare the mascarpone cream. In a large bowl, whisk together egg yolks and sugar until they become pale and thick. Then, gently fold in the mascarpone cheese until the mixture is smooth. In a separate bowl, whip the heavy cream to stiff peaks and fold it into the mascarpone mixture. (C) Next, assemble the layers. Briefly dip each ladyfinger biscuit into cooled strong coffee and arrange them in a single layer in a serving dish. Spread half of the mascarpone cream mixture evenly over the ladyfingers. Repeat with another layer of coffee-dipped ladyfingers and the remaining cream.

The classic baking soda and vinegar volcano is a staple of science fairs for a reason: it's a simple and dramatic demonstration of a chemical reaction. To create your own, you'll need a plastic bottle, clay or dough, baking soda, vinegar, and some red food coloring. (A) Now for the eruption. Pour a generous amount of vinegar, mixed with a few drops of red food coloring, into the bottle's opening. The mixture will immediately begin to fizz and bubble, flowing down the sides of the clay structure like lava from a real volcano. (B) The initial step is to build the volcano's structure. Place the plastic bottle in the center of a tray or pan. Then, mold the clay or dough around the bottle, forming a cone shape that resembles a mountain, leaving the opening of the bottle exposed at the top. (C) Once the structure is complete, you need to add the base reactant. Carefully spoon a few tablespoons of baking soda directly into the plastic bottle through the opening at the top. This is the chemical that will react with the acid to create the 'eruption'.

My one-day trip to the historic city of Gyeongju was meticulously planned to cover as much ground as possible. The journey began early in the morning, arriving at the Gyeongju train station by 9 AM. (A) As the sun began to set, the final destination was Donggung Palace and Wolji Pond. The reflection of the illuminated ancient pavilions on the water was a breathtaking sight, providing a serene and beautiful end to a long day of exploration. (B) From the station, the first stop was the Bulguksa Temple, a masterpiece of Buddhist art. After spending a couple of hours admiring the intricate architecture and serene atmosphere, the next move was to the nearby Seokguram Grotto to see the magnificent stone Buddha statue. (C) After a satisfying lunch of local delicacies near Bulguksa, the afternoon itinerary was dedicated to the Daereungwon Tomb Complex. Walking among the giant, grassy royal tombs felt like stepping back in time to the Silla Dynasty.

Assembling flat-pack furniture can be a daunting task, but following the instructions carefully makes it manageable. Let's begin assembling a simple bookshelf, having already unpacked all the components and checked them against the parts list. (A) With the frame built, the next step is to slide the thin back panel into the grooves on the rear of the side panels. Once it is in place, secure it by hammering in the small nails provided, ensuring the frame is square and stable. (B) First, lay the two main side panels on the floor. Take the top, bottom, and fixed middle shelves and attach them to one of the side panels using the provided screws. Do not tighten them completely yet. (C) Now, carefully place the second side panel on top of the exposed ends of the shelves and fasten it. Once all shelves are connected to both side panels, you can go back and tighten all the screws securely to form a rigid frame.

[지문 455]

Reporting a minor traffic incident to your insurance company requires a clear and chronological account of the event. Imagine you were involved in a small fender bender in a parking lot. Here is how you might structure your report. (A) Following the initial impact, both drivers agreed to move the vehicles to a safe spot to avoid obstructing traffic. We then exchanged insurance details, phone numbers, and took several photos of the damage to both cars from multiple angles. (B) The incident occurred at approximately 3:00 PM. I was slowly reversing out of a parking space when the other vehicle, a blue sedan, turned into the lane at a higher than expected speed. The driver of the sedan did not see my vehicle in time, and its front bumper made contact with my rear bumper. (C) No injuries were reported by either party, and since the damage appeared to be minor, we decided not to call the police. The entire exchange was calm and cooperative. I contacted my insurance agent as soon as I returned home to initiate the claims process.

Vocabulary

  • chronological연대순의
  • fenderbender 경미한 접촉 사고
  • obstructing막는
  • initiate시작하다
