[지문 456]

Potting a succulent is a simple process, but getting the drainage right is key to its survival. You will need a pot with a drainage hole, special cactus or succulent soil mix, and your chosen succulent plant. (A) Next, gently remove the succulent from its original container, lightly dusting off the old soil from its roots. Place it in the center of the new pot and begin to fill the surrounding space with the soil mix, making sure the plant sits at the same depth it did in its original pot. (B) Once the plant is securely in place, you can add a decorative top layer, such as small pebbles or gravel. This not only looks nice but also helps to keep the leaves from touching the moist soil. Avoid watering it for a few days to allow the roots to heal from the transplanting process. (C) Begin by filling the bottom of the new pot with a layer of the succulent soil mix, enough to raise the base of the plant to just below the pot's rim. This initial layer ensures that the roots won't be sitting at the very bottom.

Vocabulary

  • succulent다육식물
  • drainage배수
  • pebbles조약돌
  • transplanting옮겨심기

Setting up a new smartphone for the first time involves a series of essential steps to get it running and personalized. After unboxing the device and pressing the power button, the screen will light up with a welcome message. (A) After the basic setup, the phone will prompt you to secure your device using a PIN, pattern, or biometric data like a fingerprint or face scan. This is a crucial step for protecting your personal information. Following this, you can sign in to your Google or Apple account to restore backups or sync data. (B) The first screen you interact with will ask you to select your language and region. Once you have made your selections, the next step is to connect to a Wi-Fi network. This is necessary to download system updates and sign in to your accounts. (C) With the security and accounts configured, the phone will then guide you through a few final manufacturer-specific features and agreements. Once you accept these, you will finally arrive at the home screen, ready to download your favorite apps and customize your settings.

  • prompt촉구하다

Creating a vibrant tie-dye T-shirt is a fun and creative project. The most important preliminary step is to prepare your workspace and the shirt itself. The shirt, preferably 100% cotton, must be washed and left damp. (A) Now comes the exciting part: applying the dye. Wearing gloves, apply the different colored dyes to the various sections of the folded shirt. Be generous and make sure the dye penetrates the folds. Once done, place the dyed shirt in a plastic bag and let it sit for 6-8 hours for the colors to set. (B) After the waiting period, the final step is the reveal. While still wearing gloves, take the shirt out of the bag and rinse it under cold water until the water runs clear. Then, carefully cut the rubber bands, unfold the shirt to see your design, and wash it one last time by itself in the washing machine. (C) First, lay the damp shirt flat on your covered work surface. Decide on a pattern, such as a spiral or a crumple. For a spiral, pinch the center of the shirt and twist it into a tight, flat disc. Then, secure the shape by wrapping several rubber bands around it, creating wedge-like sections.

Vocabulary

  • preliminary예비의
  • damp축축한
  • penetrates침투하다
  • rinse헹구다

[지문 459]

Performing basic first aid for a minor cut is an essential skill. Proper care can prevent infection and help the wound heal faster. The first priority is always to ensure your own hands are clean. (A) After the bleeding has stopped, the next step is to clean the wound. Gently rinse the area with cool, clean water to remove any dirt or debris. You can use a mild soap around the wound, but avoid getting soap directly into it. Pat the area dry with a clean cloth. (B) First, apply gentle and direct pressure on the cut using a clean cloth or bandage. Hold the pressure continuously for a few minutes. This action helps the blood to clot and will usually stop the bleeding for most small cuts. (C) Finally, apply a thin layer of antibiotic ointment to the wound and cover it with a sterile bandage or dressing. This will help keep the area clean, prevent infection, and provide a moist environment conducive to healing. Change the bandage daily or whenever it becomes wet or dirty.

Vocabulary

  • firstaid 응급처치
  • debris파편, 부스러기
  • ointment연고
  • sterile살균된

[지문 460]

The launch sequence of the Apollo 11 mission was a precisely timed series of events, culminating in humanity's first steps on the Moon. The process began hours before ignition at Kennedy Space Center. (A) Upon reaching the desired Earth orbit, the spacecraft circled the globe one and a half times while the crew and mission control performed final checks. Then, the third-stage engine fired again for the "trans-lunar injection," a crucial burn that sent the Apollo 11 crew on their path to the Moon. (B) At T-minus zero, the five powerful F-1 engines of the Saturn V rocket's first stage ignited, generating immense thrust. For the first two and a half minutes, these engines lifted the massive rocket off the launch pad and through the densest part of the Earth's atmosphere before separating and falling away. (C) Following the first-stage separation, the second-stage engines took over, pushing the rocket to the edge of space. This stage also burned for several minutes before it too was jettisoned, at which point the single engine of the third stage ignited to push the Apollo spacecraft into a temporary Earth orbit.

Vocabulary

  • culminatingin ~으로 절정에 달하는
  • ignition점화
  • thrust추력
  • jettisoned분리된

The distinction between traditional and digital art is a central topic in contemporary art discourse, primarily revolving around the medium and the creative process. This difference shapes not only how art is made but also how it is perceived and valued. (A) In contrast, digital art is created using software, computers, and other electronic devices. It exists as a file of data, which can be reproduced infinitely without any loss of quality. The process often involves layers, filters, and an 'undo' function, offering a different kind of creative freedom and flexibility. (B) Traditional art, for instance, is characterized by its use of physical materials like paint, clay, or charcoal. Each piece is a unique, tangible object, and the artist's physical interaction with the medium—the brushstroke, the chisel mark—is an integral part of the final work's texture and presence. (C) This fundamental difference in materiality affects not only the creation process but also concepts of originality and value. While a traditional painting is a one-of-a-kind artifact, a digital work's value is often tied to its concept or the limited edition prints made from the file.

  • tangible실체가 있는
  • infinitely무한히
  • materiality물질성

[지문 462]

Hellenistic philosophy offered various paths to achieving *eudaimonia*, or a state of flourishing and tranquility. Among the most influential schools of thought were Stoicism and Epicureanism, which proposed distinct approaches to living a good life. (A) The Stoics, on the other hand, taught that virtue is the only good and that external events like pleasure, pain, or health are indifferent. They argued that a wise person achieves tranquility by accepting fate and mastering their emotions through reason, remaining undisturbed by life's vicissitudes. (B) This contrast in philosophy led to different practical ethics. An Epicurean might seek a quiet life with friends, avoiding political turmoil, while a Stoic would feel a duty to engage in public life to practice virtue, regardless of the personal consequences. (C) Epicureanism, for example, identified pleasure as the highest good. However, this was not a call for hedonistic indulgence but for *ataraxia*—a state of serene calmness, free from fear and bodily pain. This was best achieved by living modestly and gaining knowledge of the world.

Vocabulary

  • flourishing번영
  • hedonistic쾌락주의적인
  • vicissitudes우여곡절
  • turmoil혼란

[지문 463]

The Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect is a phenomenon where metropolitan areas are significantly warmer than their surrounding rural areas. This temperature difference is caused by a combination of factors related to urban development. (A) Furthermore, the lack of vegetation in cities reduces the natural cooling effect of evapotranspiration. Trees and green spaces provide shade and release water vapor, which cools the air, but these are often scarce in densely built environments. (B) The primary cause is the modification of land surfaces. Urban materials like concrete, asphalt, and brick have lower albedo (reflectivity) and higher heat capacity than natural landscapes. They absorb and store more solar radiation during the day and release it slowly at night. (C) Together, these factors create a dome of warm air over cities, leading to consequences such as increased energy consumption for air conditioning, higher levels of air pollution, and heat-related health problems for residents.

Vocabulary

  • metropolitan대도시의
  • albedo알베도, 반사율
  • evapotranspiration증발산
  • dome돔, 반구형 지붕
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 464]

In psychology, memory is not a single, monolithic entity but a complex system composed of different types. These types are generally classified based on the duration for which information is stored and the nature of the information itself. (A) If information from short-term memory is rehearsed or deemed important, it can be encoded into long-term memory. This vast repository holds information for extended periods, from days to a lifetime, and includes everything from personal experiences to factual knowledge. (B) Following this initial stage is short-term memory, which acts as a temporary workspace. It can hold a small amount of information (typically around 7 items) for a brief period (about 20-30 seconds) unless it is actively maintained through repetition. (C) The process begins with sensory memory, the briefest form of memory. It acts as a buffer for stimuli received through the senses, holding a large amount of information for a mere fraction of a second, just long enough for it to be processed.

Vocabulary

  • monolithic단일체의
  • sensory감각의
  • rehearsed되풀이된
  • repository저장소

[지문 465]

The transition from Renaissance to Baroque art in the 17th century marked a significant stylistic shift in Europe. While Baroque art grew out of the techniques and ideals of the High Renaissance, it developed its own distinct characteristics, often in response to profound social and religious changes. (A) In contrast, Baroque art, which flourished during the Counter-Reformation, embraced drama, emotion, and grandeur. Artists like Caravaggio and Bernini used intense light and shadow (chiaroscuro), dynamic compositions, and emotionally charged moments to create a direct and powerful impact on the viewer. (B) This stylistic divergence can be linked to their differing contexts. The rational clarity of the Renaissance reflected humanist ideals, while the dramatic intensity of the Baroque was often employed by the Catholic Church to inspire awe and piety in the faithful. (C) Renaissance art of the 15th and 16th centuries, for instance, is known for its harmony, balance, and clarity. Masters such as Leonardo da Vinci and Raphael created works that were rational and ordered, emphasizing idealized human forms and stable, symmetrical compositions.

Vocabulary

  • stylistic양식의
  • grandeur웅장함
  • divergence분기, 차이
  • piety경건함

The invention of the printing press by Johannes Gutenberg in the mid-15th century was a watershed moment in human history. However, its impact was not instantaneous but unfolded over decades, fundamentally reshaping society through the dissemination of information. (A) Consequently, literacy rates, which had been confined to the clergy and nobility, began to rise among the merchant class and even common people. This new readership created a demand for books in vernacular languages, not just Latin, further democratizing knowledge. (B) Initially, the primary use of the press was for religious texts, most notably the Bible. This allowed for the rapid spread of religious ideas, which played a crucial role in fueling the Protestant Reformation by allowing individuals to interpret scriptures for themselves. (C) As the technology became more widespread and affordable, its application expanded beyond religion. Universities and scholars used it to mass-produce academic texts, while political pamphlets and news sheets began to circulate, informing and mobilizing the public on a scale never before possible.

Vocabulary

  • watershed분수령
  • instantaneous즉각적인
  • vernacular자국의, 토착의

The concept of a "flow state," as proposed by psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, describes a mental state of being fully immersed in an activity. It is often characterized as the optimal experience, where individuals perform at their peak and feel a deep sense of enjoyment. (A) The subjective experience of being in this state is profound. Individuals often report a loss of self-consciousness, a distorted sense of time (where hours can feel like minutes), and a feeling of control over their actions. The activity becomes autotelic, meaning it is intrinsically rewarding. (B) Achieving this state, however, is not accidental. It requires a specific set of conditions, most notably a clear set of goals, immediate feedback on progress, and a fine balance between the challenge of the task and the skill level of the individual. (C) The benefits of regularly experiencing flow are significant. Beyond the immediate enjoyment, it is linked to increased creativity, higher levels of performance, and long-term happiness and personal growth, as individuals push their abilities to new limits.

Vocabulary

  • immersed몰입된
  • optimal최적의
  • autotelic자기 목적적인
  • intrinsically본질적으로

[지문 468]

The difference between direct current (DC) and alternating current (AC) is fundamental to understanding electricity. While both are movements of electric charge, the manner of this movement dictates their respective applications and advantages. (A) This unidirectional flow makes it ideal for electronics that require a steady, low-voltage power supply, such as batteries, solar cells, and most digital devices. However, transmitting it over long distances is inefficient due to significant energy loss. (B) In contrast, alternating current periodically reverses its direction, oscillating back and forth. This characteristic allows its voltage to be easily stepped up or down using transformers, making it highly efficient for long-distance power transmission from power plants to homes. (C) Direct current, as its name implies, flows in only one direction. It moves from the negative terminal to the positive terminal of a power source in a constant, straight path.

Vocabulary

  • directcurrent (DC) 직류
  • alternatingcurrent (AC) 교류
  • unidirectional단방향의
  • oscillating진동하는
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 469]

Mycorrhizal networks, often dubbed the "Wood Wide Web," represent a fascinating symbiotic relationship in forest ecosystems. These underground networks are formed by fungi that connect the roots of individual plants, including trees. (A) Through these fungal threads, trees can exchange vital resources. For example, a well-lit tree can send excess carbon to a shaded neighbor, while plants can share water and nutrients like nitrogen and phosphorus, enhancing the survival of the entire community. (B) This intricate web is not just a passive pipeline for resources. Research has shown that trees can also use it to send chemical warning signals to each other about insect attacks, allowing neighboring trees to mount their defenses preemptively. (C) The basis of this relationship is a trade: the fungus colonizes the tree's roots, providing it with increased access to water and soil nutrients, and in return, the tree provides the fungus with sugars produced through photosynthesis.

Vocabulary

  • mycorrhizal균근의
  • symbiotic공생의
  • colonizes군락을 이루다
  • preemptively선제적으로
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 470]

The classification of volcanic eruptions is largely based on their explosivity, which is determined by the magma's composition. Specifically, the viscosity and gas content of the magma dictate whether an eruption will be gentle or violent. (A) In stark contrast, explosive eruptions are driven by high-viscosity magma, which is thick and sticky like paste. This magma traps gases, causing immense pressure to build up. When this pressure is released, it results in a violent, catastrophic explosion that can hurl ash and rock kilometers into the atmosphere. (B) This difference in eruptive style directly shapes the volcano's structure. The gentle, widespread lava flows of effusive eruptions build broad, gently sloping shield volcanoes, while the alternating layers of lava and ash from explosive eruptions create steep-sided stratovolcanoes. (C) Effusive eruptions, for example, involve low-viscosity magma that flows easily, like honey. Because gases can escape from this fluid lava with ease, these eruptions are relatively calm, characterized by fountains and steady flows of lava that spread over large areas.

Vocabulary

  • explosivity폭발성
  • viscosity점성
  • effusive용출성의
  • stratovolcanoes성층화산

[지문 471]

How does George Orwell's allegory of a farm in *Animal Farm* critique the ideals and ultimate failure of the Russian Revolution? The novel presents a seemingly simple story of farm animals, but it functions as a powerful political satire. (A) This initial phase of the rebellion mirrors the early optimism of the Russian Revolution, where the working class sought to establish a society free from the tyranny of the ruling class. The Seven Commandments of Animalism are established, embodying principles of equality and justice. (B) However, this utopian vision quickly deteriorates. The pigs, being the most intelligent animals, gradually assume control. Led by the cunning and ruthless Napoleon, they begin to alter the commandments to suit their own interests, exploit the labor of other animals, and eliminate any opposition, reflecting the rise of Stalin and his totalitarian regime. (C) The story begins with the animals of Manor Farm, inspired by the ideals of a pig named Old Major, overthrowing their human farmer, Mr. Jones. They aim to create a self-sufficient farm where all animals are equal and free.

Vocabulary

  • allegory풍자, 비유
  • tyranny폭정
  • utopian유토피아적인

What makes the visual language of Christopher Nolan's film *Inception* so groundbreaking in its depiction of dreams? The film is celebrated not just for its complex plot but for its innovative visual representation of the subconscious mind. (A) A prime example is the iconic hallway fight scene. Here, the dream world's physics are manipulated, allowing for a combat sequence in a rotating, zero-gravity environment. This was achieved through a combination of practical effects, using a massive rotating set, and seamless CGI, creating a visually stunning and disorienting experience. (B) The core of this visual language lies in the concept of dream "architecture." Characters in the film don't just enter dreams; they build them. This leads to surreal cityscapes that can be folded in on themselves, streets that defy gravity, and structures that shatter like glass, visually externalizing the limitless yet fragile nature of the dream world. (C) Ultimately, these visual elements are not mere spectacle. They serve the narrative by making the abstract concept of layered consciousness tangible. The labyrinthine, physics-defying dreamscapes mirror the complexity of the human mind, reinforcing the film's central themes of reality, memory, and perception.

Vocabulary

  • surreal초현실적인
  • disorienting방향을 잃게 하는

Why is Queen's "Bohemian Rhapsody" widely considered a masterpiece of musical innovation that transcended the conventions of its time? Its enduring legacy stems from its radical departure from standard song structures. (A) This structural audacity is matched by its harmonic complexity. The operatic section, in particular, features intricate multi-tracked vocal harmonies that mimic a full choir. The dramatic shifts in key and tempo throughout this section were virtually unprecedented in mainstream rock music, showcasing the band's technical virtuosity and creative ambition. (B) First and foremost, the song defies the typical verse-chorus format of popular music. Instead, it is a six-minute suite composed of distinct movements: an a cappella introduction, a ballad segment, a guitar solo, an operatic passage, a hard rock section, and a reflective outro. This multi-part composition alone was a revolutionary act. (C) In conclusion, it was this bold fusion of disparate genres—from opera to hard rock—within a single, cohesive track that cemented its status. "Bohemian Rhapsody" challenged the creative limits of what a pop song could be and redefined the possibilities of studio recording, influencing countless artists for generations.

Vocabulary

  • transcended초월했다
  • audacity대담함
  • virtuosity기교
  • disparate이질적인

What powerful emotions and complex ideas does Vincent van Gogh convey through the dynamic composition and vibrant color of his masterpiece, *The Starry Night*? The painting is far more than a simple depiction of a night sky. (A) Beyond these visual elements, the painting is rich with symbolism. The immense, swirling celestial bodies can be interpreted as symbols of the divine or a powerful, uncontrollable nature. In stark contrast, the quiet village below, with its orderly church spire, represents the tranquil, perhaps mundane, human world. The fiery cypress tree in the foreground is often seen as a bridge between earth and heaven, symbolizing death and eternal life. (B) The initial emotional impact comes from its composition and color palette. The sky is not calm but a turbulent vortex of energy, rendered in intense blues and yellows with thick, swirling brushstrokes. This creates an overwhelming sense of cosmic drama and inner turmoil. (C) Ultimately, by prioritizing his emotional response over objective reality, Van Gogh created a quintessential work of Post-Impressionism. The painting serves as a profound expression of his spiritual longing and psychological struggles, cementing its status as an icon of modern art that powerfully visualizes the inner landscape of the human soul.

Vocabulary

  • convey전달하다
  • turbulent격동의

How does Mary Shelley's *Frankenstein* serve as a profound cautionary tale about the dangers of unchecked scientific ambition? The novel delves into this theme through the tragic story of its protagonist, Victor Frankenstein. (A) This initial act of abandonment is the critical turning point. The creature, shunned by society and its creator, is filled with loneliness and rage. Its subsequent acts of violence are not born of innate evil but are a direct consequence of Victor’s failure to take responsibility for his creation, demonstrating how ambition without morality leads to monstrous outcomes. (B) The narrative begins by establishing Victor's obsessive ambition. Consumed by a desire to "pioneer a new way" and unlock the secrets of life, he dedicates himself to creating a sentient being from non-living matter. At this stage, his ambition is portrayed as a noble, albeit reckless, pursuit of knowledge and glory. (C) In the end, Victor's ambition brings him not the fame he craved but complete ruin. He loses everyone he loves at the hands of his creation and dies a broken man, hunted by the very being he brought to life. The novel thus concludes its warning: the pursuit of knowledge without ethical consideration and compassion is a path to self-destruction.

How does director Bong Joon-ho use spatial design and architecture to critique class inequality in his film *Parasite*? The film masterfully employs the physical settings of its characters' homes as a powerful metaphor for social hierarchy. (A) In stark contrast is the Park family’s home. Perched high on a hill, this sleek, modernist mansion represents wealth, aspiration, and seclusion. Its expansive windows and manicured garden create a sense of openness, yet it is fortified by walls and gates, physically embodying the elevated and isolated status of the upper class. The architecture itself enforces the social divide. (B) The film’s message culminates when these two worlds violently collide. The devastating flood that destroys the Kims’ home while barely affecting the Parks’ highlights the brutal reality of their differing positions. The final, tragic confrontation occurs within the seemingly perfect Park house, proving that the physical and social structures separating the classes are inherently unstable and destined to erupt. (C) The critique begins with the Kim family’s semi-basement apartment. This cramped, subterranean dwelling, with its window looking up at street level, visually represents their low social standing and limited prospects. The constant threat of flooding and the smell that clings to them are sensory details that reinforce their inescapable position at the bottom of the social ladder.

Vocabulary

  • spatial공간의
  • subterranean지하의
  • manicured잘 다듬어진
  • culminates절정에 달하다
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 477]

What made Miles Davis's 1959 album *Kind of Blue* such a revolutionary moment in the history of jazz? Its seismic impact came from a fundamental shift in the approach to improvisation. (A) A perfect illustration of this is the album's opening track, "So What." The entire piece is based on just two scales, or modes. This simple harmonic foundation gave soloists like John Coltrane and Cannonball Adderley unprecedented freedom to explore melodic ideas with a new sense of space and lyricism, creating a sound that was both coolly detached and deeply expressive. (B) This new approach was modal jazz. Instead of navigating the complex and fast-moving chord progressions typical of bebop, Davis encouraged his musicians to improvise over a series of static scales, or "modes." This seemingly simple change liberated soloists from the harmonic constraints of the song's structure. (C) Consequently, *Kind of Blue* did more than just produce beautiful music; it changed the very language of jazz. It became the best-selling jazz album of all time and its emphasis on mood, space, and melodic invention influenced not only subsequent generations of jazz musicians but also artists in rock, pop, and ambient music, marking a true paradigm shift.

Vocabulary

  • seismic엄청난
  • improvisation즉흥연주
  • modaljazz 모달 재즈
  • lyricism서정성
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 478]

How does Pablo Picasso's monumental painting *Guernica* function as one of history's most powerful anti-war statements? Its enduring power lies in its ability to transform a specific historical event into a universal symbol of human suffering. (A) Within this chaotic composition, Picasso deploys a host of potent symbols. The gored bull represents brute force or fascism, while the screaming horse embodies suffering. Key figures like the mother clutching her dead child and the woman holding a lamp are universal archetypes of grief and the search for truth amidst darkness. These symbols elevate the scene from a mere report to a profound allegory. (B) The painting's immediate impact stems from its stark visual language. Picasso deliberately rejected color, using a monochromatic palette of black, white, and gray to evoke the grim reality of a newspaper photograph and the somberness of death. Its massive scale—nearly 3.5 meters tall and 7.8 meters wide—engulfs the viewer, making the horror inescapable. (C) Created in response to the 1937 bombing of a Basque town by Nazi aircraft during the Spanish Civil War, the painting's political message is undeniable. By universalizing the agony of the victims, *Guernica* transcends its specific context. It has become an enduring emblem of the atrocities of war, a timeless protest against violence that continues to resonate globally.

Vocabulary

  • monumental기념비적인
  • stark극명한
  • potent강력한
  • atrocities잔학 행위

[지문 479]

In what way does the symbol of the green light in F. Scott Fitzgerald's *The Great Gatsby* encapsulate the novel's central themes of hope, illusion, and the American Dream? This single, distant light is arguably the novel's most significant symbol. (A) As the story unfolds, however, the light’s meaning broadens. It evolves from a specific object of desire into a more abstract symbol for the American Dream itself—the widespread belief in the possibility of a prosperous and happy future that one can achieve through ambition and reinvention. Gatsby’s pursuit of Daisy becomes a metaphor for this larger, quintessentially American quest. (B) Initially, the green light is presented as something deeply personal to Jay Gatsby. Located at the end of Daisy Buchanan's dock across the bay, it represents his intense, singular longing for her and his dream of recapturing their past. For him, it is a tangible focal point for an intangible desire. (C) In the novel's poignant conclusion, the symbol's ultimate, tragic meaning is revealed. Narrator Nick Carraway reflects that the green light signifies an idealized future that is forever out of reach, a dream that we chase "ceaselessly into the past." It comes to represent the beautiful but ultimately hollow and unattainable nature of the very dreams that drive us.

Vocabulary

  • encapsulate요약하다
  • longing갈망
  • quintessentially본질적으로
  • poignant가슴 아픈
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 480]

How does Stanley Kubrick's *2001: A Space Odyssey* use a non-traditional narrative and minimal dialogue to explore humanity's evolution and its complex relationship with technology? The film famously rejects conventional storytelling to create a contemplative cinematic experience. (A) This unconventional structure frames human history as a series of great evolutionary leaps. The film opens with the "Dawn of Man," showing apes discovering the use of tools, and then abruptly jumps millions of years into the future of space travel. In each era, the mysterious black monolith appears, seemingly catalyzing the next stage of human development. (B) The film's central and most famous sequence, featuring the sentient computer HAL 9000, exemplifies this method. The conflict between man and machine unfolds not through heated arguments but through long, quiet shots, the cold hum of the ship, and HAL's unnervingly calm voice. This lack of dialogue amplifies the tension and highlights the theme of technology becoming dangerously autonomous and inhuman. (C) By deliberately minimizing dialogue and focusing on powerful visuals set to classical music, Kubrick shifts the film's purpose. It becomes less a plot-driven story and more a philosophical meditation. The audience is forced to interpret the vast, silent spaces and ambiguous events, contemplating humanity's place in the cosmos and the double-edged nature of its own creations.

Vocabulary

  • contemplative사색적인
  • monolith모놀리스, 거석
  • sentient지각이 있는
  • meditation명상
Vera's Flavor 편입영어 독해 Bridge 편
[지문 481]

In the civil case of *Thompson v. AutoCorp*, the plaintiff, Mr. Thompson, alleged that a manufacturing defect in his new vehicle's braking system directly caused his accident. The court was tasked with determining the validity of this claim. (A) Based on this legal standard, the court then examined the evidence. Expert testimony confirmed that the brake line was composed of a substandard alloy prone to failure under normal operating pressures. This established a clear breach of the manufacturer's duty. (B) The foundational legal principle in product liability cases is the "duty of care," which requires a manufacturer to ensure their products are reasonably safe for their intended use. A failure to meet this standard constitutes negligence. (C) Consequently, the court found in favor of the plaintiff. The final judgment held AutoCorp liable for damages, including medical costs and vehicle repairs, concluding that the defective brake system was the proximate cause of the accident.

Vocabulary

  • plaintiff원고
  • alleged주장했다
  • substandard표준 이하의
  • proximatecause 근접 원인

[지문 482]

A 45-year-old patient presented at the clinic with a unique constellation of symptoms: intermittent joint pain, persistent fatigue, and a distinctive skin rash that worsened with sun exposure. The initial physical examination was inconclusive. (A) To address this, a treatment plan was formulated. It includes a course of immunosuppressants to manage the autoimmune response, alongside lifestyle recommendations such as strict sun protection and a modified diet to reduce inflammation. (B) Given these findings, the diagnosis was confirmed as Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE), an autoimmune disorder where the body's immune system mistakenly attacks its own tissues. The combination of specific antibodies and clinical symptoms was definitive. (C) Therefore, a series of diagnostic tests were ordered. Blood work was conducted to screen for specific autoantibodies, particularly antinuclear antibodies (ANA), and a biopsy of the skin lesion was taken for histopathological analysis. The results were highly indicative.

Vocabulary

  • constellation무리, 집합체
  • inconclusive결정적이지 않은
  • autoantibodies자가항체
  • immunosuppressants면역억제제

A metropolitan authority initiated a project to construct a new pedestrian bridge over a busy six-lane highway. The primary objective was to ensure safe passage for residents to a newly developed park and commercial area. (A) To overcome these challenges, the engineering team proposed an innovative cable-stayed design. This design minimizes the ground footprint by using a single central pylon, and its aerodynamic profile is engineered to effectively manage wind loads, ensuring structural integrity. (B) The project's initial phase involved a thorough site analysis, which revealed several critical engineering challenges. The soil on the west bank was found to be unstable, requiring deep foundations, and wind tunnel simulations indicated unexpectedly high lateral wind forces. (C) With the design finalized, the next phase involves securing permits and beginning the foundation work. The project timeline estimates completion within 24 months, promising a vital new link for the community.

Vocabulary

  • pedestrian보행자의
  • siteanalysis 부지 분석
  • cable-stayed사장교의
  • pylon주탑

A management consulting firm was hired by "Vintage Threads," a fashion retailer experiencing a consistent decline in in-store sales over the past three years. The firm's objective was to identify the root causes and propose a viable turnaround strategy. (A) Based on this diagnosis, the firm recommended a two-pronged strategy. First, a major investment in developing a user-friendly e-commerce platform to capture the online market. Second, the implementation of a customer loyalty program to improve retention of the existing customer base. (B) The initial step was a comprehensive market and internal data analysis. The findings were stark: the target demographic had significantly shifted their purchasing habits to online retailers, and the company's lack of a digital presence was a critical failure. Furthermore, customer retention rates were alarmingly low. (C) The final report outlined a detailed implementation roadmap. It scheduled the e-commerce platform launch for the third quarter, followed by a phased rollout of the loyalty program in the fourth quarter, with clear key performance indicators (KPIs) to track progress.

[지문 485]

Recent anthropological studies have focused on the evolving nature of kinship in modern, industrialized societies. The traditional definition, centered on blood and marriage, appears to be expanding to include other forms of social bonding. (A) Another compelling case is found among urban professionals who form "work families." These groups share meals, celebrate personal milestones, and provide emotional and professional support, effectively functioning as a surrogate kin network in high-stress environments. (B) For instance, researchers observed communities of elderly individuals in retirement homes who have formed deep, familial-like bonds. These chosen families, unrelated by blood, provide crucial daily support and companionship, filling the void left by geographically distant relatives. (C) These examples collectively suggest a redefinition of kinship. It is evolving from a purely biological and legal construct to a more flexible concept based on shared experience, mutual support, and emotional connection, reflecting the changing social structures of our time.

Vocabulary

  • kinship친족 관계
  • familial-like가족과 같은
  • surrogate대리의
  • construct구성 개념

An archaeological team began an excavation at a newly discovered site in the Anatolian plains, believed to be a settlement from the early Bronze Age. The initial survey indicated the presence of buried structures. (A) Analysis of these artifacts in the lab provided critical data. Carbon dating of organic residue on the pottery placed the settlement firmly in the 2,500 BCE range. Furthermore, the metallurgy of the bronze tools suggested a sophisticated knowledge of smelting not previously attributed to cultures in this region. (B) The first phase of the dig focused on a central mound. Here, the team carefully unearthed the stone foundations of what appeared to be a large communal building and several smaller dwellings. Numerous pottery shards and fragments of bronze tools were recovered from this area. (C) Based on this combined evidence—the monumental nature of the central building and the advanced metallurgy—the lead archaeologist hypothesized that the site was not merely a simple settlement, but a regional center for trade and metal production.

In response to public concern, an environmental agency launched a comprehensive study on the declining biodiversity in the Maple Creek ecosystem. The primary goal was to identify the stressors and formulate a remediation plan. (A) Subsequent investigation focused on the source of these pollutants. Water flow and soil sample analysis traced the highest concentrations to runoff from the adjacent Northwood agricultural zone, where the use of specific pesticides and fertilizers was widespread. (B) The initial fieldwork involved systematic sampling of water, soil, and local fauna over a six-month period. The data revealed dangerously high levels of nitrates and specific pesticides in the water, correlating with a sharp decline in native amphibian populations. (C) The final report, therefore, recommends a two-part action plan. It calls for establishing a buffer zone between the farms and the creek, and initiating a program to reintroduce native plant species that can naturally filter these pollutants.

  • stressors스트레스 요인

A market research report analyzed the key factors behind several highly successful product launches in the consumer technology sector over the past year. The aim was to distill a general principle for future marketing strategies. (A) A second case examined was the launch of "Echo," a smart home device. Instead of a traditional ad campaign, the company provided early versions to influential tech bloggers and YouTubers, generating authentic, user-driven reviews and tutorials that built credibility. (B) The common denominator in these successes is a shift away from top-down messaging. The core principle is to empower a core group of early adopters or influencers to become advocates, creating a more organic and trustworthy marketing narrative. (C) For example, the "Nova" smartphone campaign did not focus on television commercials. Instead, it created an invitation-only online forum where tech enthusiasts could discuss the phone's features pre-launch, fostering a sense of exclusivity and community.

The architectural philosophy of Luis Barragán, a Pritzker Prize laureate, is renowned for its unique synthesis of modernism and traditional Mexican aesthetics. His work is often studied for its masterful use of color, light, and space. (A) This philosophy is vividly expressed in his residential projects, such as the Gilardi House. Here, he used bold planes of pink and yellow not merely as decoration, but to define spaces and evoke specific emotional responses, manipulating natural light as it moves through the interiors during the day. (B) He believed that architecture should be a vessel for "emotional silence" and serenity, a stark contrast to the purely functional and machine-like ethos of mainstream European modernism. For him, a building was incomplete without an element of mystery and poetry. (C) Consequently, his approach has had a lasting influence on a generation of architects, particularly in Latin America. They have adopted his emphasis on emotional resonance and cultural context, proving that modern architecture can be both minimalist and deeply expressive.

  • synthesis종합

A new clinical trial was designed to test the efficacy of a novel drug, "Cardia-7," intended to lower LDL cholesterol levels more effectively than existing statins. The trial enrolled 500 patients with a history of high cholesterol. (A) The results were statistically significant. The group receiving Cardia-7 showed an average LDL reduction of 55%, compared to a 35% reduction in the statin group. Furthermore, the incidence of side effects like muscle pain was notably lower in the Cardia-7 group. (B) The participants were randomly divided into two groups. The experimental group received a daily dose of Cardia-7, while the control group received a standard, widely-used statin. Both groups were monitored over a period of one year, with cholesterol levels checked quarterly. (C) Based on these promising findings, the pharmaceutical company is now preparing a New Drug Application (NDA) for submission to the regulatory authorities. The data suggests Cardia-7 could represent a major advancement in cardiovascular disease prevention.

  • statins스타틴(콜레스테롤 저하제)

[지문 491]

The Thirty Years' War (1618-1648) is often oversimplified as a purely religious conflict between Protestants and Catholics. However, its origins were far more complex, weaving together a tapestry of political, dynastic, and territorial ambitions that ultimately engulfed most of Europe. (A) These political tensions were exacerbated by the ambitions of the Habsburg dynasty, which sought to enhance its imperial authority over the German states. This desire for centralization clashed with the princes of the Holy Roman Empire, who fiercely guarded their autonomy. (B) The initial spark was indeed religious. The Defenestration of Prague in 1618, where Protestant officials threw Catholic representatives of the Emperor from a castle window, was a direct response to the perceived erosion of religious freedoms granted earlier. (C) Furthermore, foreign powers like France, Denmark, and Sweden intervened not primarily for religious reasons, but to curb the power of the Habsburgs and gain territory. France, a Catholic nation, notably supported the Protestant side to counter its rival, demonstrating the primacy of politics over religion.

Vocabulary

  • dynastic왕조의
  • engulfed휩싸인
  • Defenestration창밖으로 던지기
  • primacy우위

[지문 492]

The 2008 global financial crisis was not a sudden event but the culmination of various interconnected factors that had been developing for years. At its core was the collapse of the housing market in the United States, but this collapse itself had deeper roots. (A) This widespread default was possible because of a preceding period of deregulation in the financial industry. Banks were allowed to take on more risk, and the creation of complex financial products, such as mortgage-backed securities (MBS), was encouraged. (B) These risky products were then sold to investors around the globe, often with misleadingly high credit ratings. When the underlying mortgages began to fail, the value of these securities plummeted, spreading the crisis from the U.S. housing market to the entire global financial system. (C) The housing bubble was fueled by the proliferation of subprime mortgages, which were loans extended to borrowers with poor credit histories. For a time, rising house prices masked the risk, but when prices stagnated, many borrowers began to default on their loans.

Vocabulary

  • culmination정점
  • deregulation규제 완화
  • plummeted급락했다
  • stagnated침체되었다

[지문 493]

The destruction of the Amazon rainforest is a critical environmental issue driven by a complex web of economic activities. While logging often receives significant attention, it is only one piece of a much larger puzzle of deforestation drivers. (A) In addition, large-scale infrastructure projects, such as the construction of highways and dams, open up previously remote areas of the forest. This new access facilitates further deforestation by making it easier for various actors to penetrate deeper into the jungle. (B) The primary driver, responsible for the majority of deforestation, is cattle ranching. Vast areas of forest are cleared to create pastureland for beef production, much of which is exported to international markets, linking local land use to global consumption. (C) Following closely behind cattle ranching is the expansion of industrial agriculture, particularly the cultivation of soybeans. As global demand for soy for animal feed has surged, so has the pressure to convert forest land into massive monoculture plantations.

Vocabulary

  • logging벌목
  • pastureland목초지
  • cultivation경작
  • monoculture단일 경작

[지문 494]

The understanding of major depressive disorder has evolved significantly, moving away from simplistic explanations. Modern psychology often utilizes the biopsychosocial model, which posits that depression arises from a complex interplay of various causal factors rather than a single source. (A) Psychologically, factors such as negative thought patterns, low self-esteem, and past trauma can create a vulnerability to depression. Cognitive theories, for instance, suggest that individuals who habitually interpret events in a pessimistic way are more likely to develop depressive symptoms. (B) From a biological perspective, genetics play a crucial role, as individuals with a family history of depression are at a higher risk. Furthermore, imbalances in neurotransmitters like serotonin and dopamine in the brain are strongly correlated with the mood regulation difficulties seen in the disorder. (C) Finally, social and environmental factors, including chronic stress, social isolation, poverty, and lack of a support system, can trigger or exacerbate depression. These external pressures can overwhelm an individual's coping mechanisms, contributing to the onset of the illness.

Vocabulary

  • etiology병인학
  • interplay상호작용
  • vulnerability취약성
  • exacerbate악화시키다

[지문 495]

The significant increase in urban crime rates observed in many Western nations during the late 20th century cannot be attributed to a single cause. Sociologists point to a confluence of profound socioeconomic transformations that reshaped urban landscapes and community structures. (A) This economic dislocation led to heightened social inequality and concentrated poverty in inner-city neighborhoods. The lack of viable economic opportunities and the decay of community institutions created an environment where crime could flourish as an alternative, albeit destructive, survival strategy. (B) A primary factor was deindustrialization, the decline of manufacturing industries that had once been the bedrock of urban employment. As factories closed or relocated, large numbers of low-skilled workers, particularly men, lost stable, well-paying jobs. (C) Compounding this issue was the phenomenon of "white flight," where middle-class and wealthier residents moved to suburban areas. This exodus eroded the tax base of cities, leading to underfunded public services like schools and police, further weakening the social fabric.

Vocabulary

  • confluence합류, 융합
  • deindustrialization탈산업화
  • dislocation혼란
  • exodus탈출

[지문 496]

The fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 AD was not a singular event but a long, slow decline resulting from a combination of internal decay and external pressures. Historians identify several critical, interlocking factors that contributed to its eventual collapse. (A) Simultaneously, the empire faced relentless external threats from Germanic tribes and other "barbarian" groups along its vast borders. Constant warfare drained the treasury and manpower, forcing Rome to rely on non-Roman mercenaries whose loyalty was often questionable. (B) Internally, the empire was plagued by severe economic instability. Debasement of currency to pay for military expenses led to rampant inflation, while heavy taxation impoverished the farming class and disrupted agricultural production, weakening the economic backbone of the state. (C) This economic distress was coupled with political corruption and instability. A rapid succession of weak and ineffective emperors, often rising and falling through civil war or assassination, prevented any consistent long-term policy and eroded public confidence in the government.

Vocabulary

  • interlocking서로 맞물리는
  • debasement품질 저하
  • rampant만연하는
  • mercenaries용병

[지문 497]

The global rise in Type 2 diabetes presents a major public health challenge. Its etiology is understood to be multifactorial, involving a complex interaction between an individual's genetic makeup and a range of environmental and lifestyle influences. (A) However, genetic predisposition alone does not determine one's fate. Lifestyle choices play a profoundly significant role. A diet high in processed foods, sugar, and unhealthy fats, combined with a sedentary lifestyle lacking regular physical activity, greatly increases the risk of developing the condition. (B) First, there is a clear genetic component. Certain genes can make an individual more susceptible to insulin resistance, a key feature of Type 2 diabetes where the body's cells do not respond effectively to insulin. This inherited vulnerability sets the stage for the disease. (C) Furthermore, socioeconomic determinants can create an environment that fosters these unhealthy lifestyles. Limited access to affordable healthy food, unsafe neighborhoods for exercise, and chronic stress associated with poverty can all contribute indirectly to the rising prevalence of diabetes.

Vocabulary

  • etiology병인학
  • multifactorial다요인의
  • predisposition소인, 성향
  • sedentary앉아서 하는

[지문 498]

The bursting of the dot-com bubble in 2000-2001 was a dramatic market correction that wiped out trillions of dollars in market value. This collapse was not caused by a single misstep but by a perfect storm of speculative fervor, flawed business models, and market overconfidence. (A) This speculative mania was fueled by the media and market analysts who hyped the "new economy," encouraging investment in any company with a ".com" in its name, regardless of its actual revenue or a clear path to profitability. (B) At the heart of the bubble was a massive wave of venture capital investment into internet-based startups. Many of these companies were built on flimsy business models, prioritizing rapid growth in "eyeballs" or web traffic over sustainable revenue streams. (C) Eventually, reality set in. As many of these dot-coms continued to burn through cash without turning a profit, investor confidence began to wane. A few high-profile failures triggered a panic, leading to a massive sell-off as investors rushed to exit the market before their holdings became worthless.

  • correction조정
  • speculativefervor 투기 열풍

[지문 499]

The outbreak of the Syrian Civil War in 2011 had deep and complex roots that extended far beyond the initial Arab Spring protests. A convergence of long-term socioeconomic grievances, political repression, and environmental pressures created a highly volatile situation. (A) Compounding these issues was a severe, multi-year drought that devastated Syria's agricultural heartland between 2006 and 2010. This environmental crisis displaced over a million farmers, driving them into already overcrowded and impoverished urban centers and heightening social tensions. (B) For decades, the country was ruled by an authoritarian regime that suppressed political dissent and concentrated wealth and power within a small elite. This led to widespread feelings of injustice and marginalization among large segments of the population. (C) On top of this political discontent, growing socioeconomic inequality was a major source of frustration. Neoliberal economic reforms had benefited a select few connected to the regime, while youth unemployment soared and public services deteriorated for the majority.
